what: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: Unified network estimation, analysis, validation behavioral, psychological, panel data. Nestimate comprehensive R package estimating, validating, comparing networks behavioral sequence data, psychological scales, longitudinal panel data. single entry point — build_network() — dispatches 13 built-estimators. Every network type shares validation pipeline: bootstrap confidence intervals, permutation testing, split-half reliability, centrality stability. entire package 4 hard imports (ggplot2, glasso, data.table, cluster).

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: Network Estimation & Visualization Higher-Order & Simplicial Complexes Model Assessment

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: Transition Networks Sequence Plots & Comparison Clustering & Multi-Level Analysis Markov Stability Sequence Pattern Comparison

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
code: # From CRAN install.packages("Nestimate")  # Development version devtools::install_github("mohsaqr/Nestimate")

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: dynamic network methods use build_network(). Pass event log action, actor, time columns — preprocessing needed.

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
code: library(Nestimate) data(human_long)  net <- build_network(human_long, method = "tna",                      action = "action", actor = "session_id", time = "time")  # Per-group networks in one call group_nets <- build_network(human_long, method = "tna",                             action = "action", actor = "session_id",                             time = "time", group = "phase")

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: wtna() builds networks binary (one-hot) data using temporal windows — directed transitions windows, undirected co-occurrence within windows, mixed network combining .
code: data(learning_activities) net_wtna  <- wtna(learning_activities, actor = "student",                   method = "transition", type = "relative") net_mixed <- wtna(learning_activities, actor = "student",                   method = "both", type = "relative")

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: cooccurrence() builds undirected co-occurrence networks 6 input formats (delimited fields, long/bipartite, binary matrix, wide sequence, lists) 8 similarity methods (Jaccard, cosine, association strength, Dice, ).
code: # From a long-format data frame net_co <- cooccurrence(human_long, field = "action", by = "session_id",                        similarity = "jaccard", threshold = 0.1)

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: implemented scratch dependency igraph, qgraph, bootnet.
code: data(srl_strategies) net_gl  <- build_network(srl_strategies, method = "glasso") net_mgm <- build_network(mixed_data, method = "mgm")   # scales + demographics  predictability(net_gl)   # R-squared per node from network structure

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: build_mlvar() estimates three networks simultaneously ESM/EMA diary data — three pillars mlVAR analysis single function call: Temporal (directed) — lagged fixed effects: predicts across time Contemporaneous (undirected) — partial correlations within-person residuals: moves together right now -subjects (undirected) — partial correlations person means: differs Machine-precision equivalence mlVAR::mlVAR() validated across 25 real ESM datasets, runs 1.45× faster.
code: data(chatgpt_srl) fit <- build_mlvar(chatgpt_srl, vars = c("planning", "monitoring", "evaluation"),                    id = "id", day = "day", beep = "beep")  fit$temporal          # directed network of lagged effects fit$contemporaneous   # undirected within-person partial correlations fit$between           # undirected between-persons partial correlations coefs(fit)            # tidy data.frame: beta, SE, t, p, CI for every edge

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: build_gimme() estimates separate network person using Group Iterative Mean Estimation (GIMME) algorithm, aggregates group-level picture. Use -person heterogeneity matters single group network average meaningfully different individuals.
code: fit_g <- build_gimme(panel_data, vars = c("x1", "x2", "x3"), id = "id") fit_g$group_network       # aggregated group-level paths fit_g$individual_networks # one network per person

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: build_clusters() partitions sequences k groups using pairwise distance matrices. Supports 9 distance metrics 8 clustering algorithms. build_clusters() build_mmm() results pass directly build_network().
code: clust <- build_clusters(net, k = 3, dissimilarity = "hamming", method = "ward.D2") plot(clust, type = "silhouette") cluster_nets <- build_network(clust, method = "tna")

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: build_mmm() discovers latent subgroups sequences share similar transition dynamics via EM — without pre-labelling groups. BIC/AIC/ICL model selection via compare_mmm().
code: mmm   <- build_mmm(net, k = 3) compare_mmm(net, k = 2:6)   # model selection plot + table mmm_nets <- build_network(mmm, method = "tna")

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: build_mcml() decomposes network macro (-cluster) micro (within-cluster) layers nodes belong known groups.
code: clusters <- list(Metacognitive = c("Planning", "Monitoring"),                  Cognitive     = c("Elaboration", "Organisation")) mcml <- cluster_summary(net, clusters) mcml$macro$weights

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: Capture dependencies beyond first-order transitions:
code: hon  <- build_hon(net, max_order = 2) pathways(hon)                      # arrow-notation path strings hypa <- build_hypa(net) hypa$over                          # over-represented paths with p-values

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: Go beyond edges — find cliques, holes, high-order connectivity using tools algebraic topology.
code: sc <- build_simplicial(net, method = "clique") betti_numbers(sc)          # connected components, cycles, voids euler_characteristic(sc) ph <- persistent_homology(net)  # track topology across thresholds plot(ph) qa <- q_analysis(sc)       # Atkin's Q-connectivity structure vectors

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: Visualize raw sequence data index plots state distribution charts — clustering.
code: # Sequence index plot: one row per person, coloured by state sequence_plot(net)  # After clustering: faceted by cluster clust <- build_clusters(net, k = 3) sequence_plot(clust, type = "index")  # State distribution over time distribution_plot(net, type = "area") distribution_plot(clust, type = "bar")

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: sequence_compare() extracts k-gram patterns grouped sequences, counts per-group frequencies, tests statistical differences via permutation — answering question “groups actually behave differently, ?”
code: data(human_long) net <- build_network(human_long, method = "tna",                      action = "action", actor = "session_id",                      time = "time", group = "phase")  res <- sequence_compare(net, sub = 2:4, test = "chisq", adjust = "fdr") res$patterns                        # per-pattern frequencies + p-values plot(res)                           # back-to-back pyramid chart plot(res, style = "heatmap")        # heatmap for many patterns

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: association_rules() mines “B” patterns sequences binary matrices using Apriori algorithm. Returns support, confidence, lift, conviction every rule threshold.
code: rules <- association_rules(net, min_support = 0.05, min_confidence = 0.6) rules$rules                       # tidy data.frame, sorted by lift pathways(rules)                   # rules as arrow-notation strings  # From a raw binary matrix rules2 <- association_rules(binary_mat, min_support = 0.1)

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: predict_links() scores unobserved node pairs using structural similarity, identifying missing connections likely exist. evaluate_links() computes AUC, precision, recall held-edges.
code: preds <- predict_links(net)       # common neighbours, Adamic-Adar, Katz, ... head(preds$scores)                # sorted by predicted score  # Evaluate against known missing edges eval  <- evaluate_links(net, held_out = test_edges) eval$auc

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: markov_stability() measures stable network partition random-walk dynamics different time scales — resolution-free way find communities. passage_time() computes expected first-passage return times states.
code: stab <- markov_stability(net, times = seq(0.1, 10, 0.1)) plot(stab)                # stability vs time-scale curve  pt <- passage_time(net) pt$first_passage          # expected steps to reach state j from state i pt$return_time            # expected steps to return to the same state

title: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis
text: Every network type shares validation pipeline.
code: # Bootstrap confidence intervals boot <- bootstrap_network(net, iter = 1000) summary(boot)  # Permutation test: are two networks different? perm <- permutation(group_nets$`Cluster 1`, group_nets$`Cluster 2`)  # Network Comparison Test (NCT): formal test of structure + global strength nct_res <- nct(data1, data2, iter = 500) print(nct_res)            # M-statistic, S-statistic, per-edge p-values  # Split-half reliability network_reliability(net)  # Centrality stability (CS-coefficient) centrality_stability(net)  # Glasso-specific bootstrap (edge inclusion + centrality CIs) boot_gl <- boot_glasso(net_pna, iter = 1000)

what: Nestimate: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis — Nestimate-package
title: Nestimate: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis — Nestimate-package
text: Estimate, compare, analyze dynamic psychological networks using unified interface. Provides transition network analysis estimation (transition, frequency, co-occurrence, attention-weighted) Saqr et al. (2025) doi:10.1145/3706468.3706513 , psychological network methods (correlation, partial correlation, 'graphical lasso', 'Ising') Saqr, Beck, Lopez-Pernas (2024) doi:10.1007/978-3-031-54464-4_19 , higher-order network methods including higher-order networks, higher-order network embedding, hyper-path anomaly, multi-order generative model. Supports bootstrap inference, permutation testing, split-half reliability, centrality stability analysis, mixed Markov models, multi-cluster multi-layer networks clustering.

title: Nestimate: Network Estimation, Bootstrap, and Higher-Order Analysis — Nestimate-package

what: Convert Action Column to One-Hot Encoding — action_to_onehot
title: Convert Action Column to One-Hot Encoding — action_to_onehot
text: Convert categorical Action column one-hot (binary indicator) columns.

title: Convert Action Column to One-Hot Encoding — action_to_onehot
code: action_to_onehot(   data,   action_col = "Action",   states = NULL,   drop_action = TRUE,   sort_states = FALSE,   prefix = "" )

title: Convert Action Column to One-Hot Encoding — action_to_onehot
text: data Data frame containing action column. action_col Character. Name action column. Default: "Action". states Character vector NULL. States include columns. NULL, uses unique values. Default: NULL. drop_action Logical. Remove original action column. Default: TRUE. sort_states Logical. Sort state columns alphabetically. Default: FALSE. prefix Character. Prefix state column names. Default: "".

title: Convert Action Column to One-Hot Encoding — action_to_onehot
text: Data frame one-hot encoded columns (0/1 integers).

title: Convert Action Column to One-Hot Encoding — action_to_onehot
code: long_data <- data.frame(   Actor = rep(1:3, each = 4),   Time = rep(1:4, 3),   Action = sample(c("A", "B", "C"), 12, replace = TRUE) ) onehot_data <- action_to_onehot(long_data) head(onehot_data) #>   Actor Time A C B #> 1     1    1 1 0 0 #> 2     1    2 0 1 0 #> 3     1    3 0 1 0 #> 4     1    4 1 0 0 #> 5     2    1 0 0 1 #> 6     2    2 0 1 0

what: Tidy per-actor endpoint summary of a wide-format sequence dataset. — actor_endpoints
title: Tidy per-actor endpoint summary of a wide-format sequence dataset. — actor_endpoints
text: actor (row), reports first last observed states, time indices appear, number observed steps, dropped_out flag TRUE actor terminal-NA pattern (final observed step, every remaining cell NA).

title: Tidy per-actor endpoint summary of a wide-format sequence dataset. — actor_endpoints
code: actor_endpoints(data, cols = NULL)

title: Tidy per-actor endpoint summary of a wide-format sequence dataset. — actor_endpoints
text: data wide-format matrix data.frame rows actors columns time steps. text: Discovers association rules using Apriori algorithm proper candidate pruning. Accepts netobject (extracts sequences transactions), data frames, lists, binary matrices. Support counting vectorized via crossprod() 2-itemsets logical matrix indexing k-itemsets.

title: Discover Association Rules from Sequential or Transaction Data — association_rules
code: association_rules(   x,   min_support = 0.1,   min_confidence = 0.5,   min_lift = 1,   max_length = 5L )

title: Discover Association Rules from Sequential or Transaction Data — association_rules
text: x Input data. Accepts: netobject Uses $data sequences — sequence becomes transaction unique states. list element character vector items (one transaction). data.frame Wide format: row transaction, character columns item occurrences. binary matrix (0/1). matrix Binary transaction matrix (rows = transactions, columns = items). min_support Numeric. Minimum support threshold. Default: 0.1. min_confidence Numeric. Minimum confidence threshold. Default: 0.5. min_lift Numeric. Minimum lift threshold. Default: 1.0. max_length Integer. Maximum itemset size. Default: 5.

title: Discover Association Rules from Sequential or Transaction Data — association_rules
text: object class "net_association_rules" containing: rules Data frame columns: antecedent (list), consequent (list), support, confidence, lift, conviction, count, n_transactions. frequent_itemsets List frequent itemsets per level k. items Character vector items. n_transactions Integer. n_rules Integer. params List min_support, min_confidence, min_lift, max_length.

title: Discover Association Rules from Sequential or Transaction Data — association_rules
text: Uses level-wise Apriori (Agrawal & Srikant, 1994) full pruning step: join step generates k-candidates, (k-1)-subsets verified frequent support counting. critical efficiency k >= 4.

title: Discover Association Rules from Sequential or Transaction Data — association_rules
text: support P(B). Fraction transactions containing antecedent consequent. confidence P(B | ). Fraction antecedent transactions also contain consequent. lift P(B) / (P() * P(B)). Values > 1 indicate positive association; < 1 indicate negative association. conviction (1 - P(B)) / (1 - confidence). Measures departure independence. Higher = stronger implication.

title: Discover Association Rules from Sequential or Transaction Data — association_rules
text: Agrawal, R. & Srikant, R. (1994). Fast algorithms mining association rules. Proc. 20th VLDB Conference, 487–499.

title: Discover Association Rules from Sequential or Transaction Data — association_rules
code: # From a list of transactions trans <- list(   c("plan", "discuss", "execute"),   c("plan", "research", "analyze"),   c("discuss", "execute", "reflect"),   c("plan", "discuss", "execute", "reflect"),   c("research", "analyze", "reflect") ) rules <- association_rules(trans, min_support = 0.3, min_confidence = 0.5) print(rules) #> Association Rules  [24 rules | 6 items | 5 transactions] #>   Support >= 0.30  |  Confidence >= 0.50  |  Lift >= 1.00 #>  #>   Top rules (by lift): #>     1. analyze -> research  (sup=0.400 conf=1.000 lift=2.50) #>     2. research -> analyze  (sup=0.400 conf=1.000 lift=2.50) #>     3. discuss -> execute  (sup=0.600 conf=1.000 lift=1.67) #>     4. execute -> discuss  (sup=0.600 conf=1.000 lift=1.67) #>     5. discuss, plan -> execute  (sup=0.400 conf=1.000 lift=1.67) #>     6. execute, plan -> discuss  (sup=0.400 conf=1.000 lift=1.67) #>     7. discuss, reflect -> execute  (sup=0.400 conf=1.000 lift=1.67) #>     8. execute, reflect -> discuss  (sup=0.400 conf=1.000 lift=1.67) #>     9. discuss -> execute, plan  (sup=0.400 conf=0.667 lift=1.67) #>     10. execute -> discuss, plan  (sup=0.400 conf=0.667 lift=1.67) #>     ... and 14 more rules  # From a netobject (sequences as transactions) seqs <- data.frame(   V1 = sample(LETTERS[1:5], 50, TRUE),   V2 = sample(LETTERS[1:5], 50, TRUE),   V3 = sample(LETTERS[1:5], 50, TRUE) ) net <- build_network(seqs, method = "relative") rules <- association_rules(net, min_support = 0.1)

what: Bayesian Dirichlet-Multinomial comparison of two transition networks — bayes_compare
title: Bayesian Dirichlet-Multinomial comparison of two transition networks — bayes_compare
text: Compares two transition networks estimated build_network (method "relative" "frequency") using Bayesian Dirichlet-Multinomial model. outgoing transitions source state modelled Multinomial draw Dirichlet prior transition probabilities. Jeffreys prior posterior transitions state \(\) \(\mathrm{Dirichlet}(c_i + \alpha)\), \(c_i\) observed outgoing counts. edge probability marginally Beta-distributed, posterior mean difference two networks available closed form credible interval obtained Monte Carlo. complement permutation: permutation test answers "difference extreme chance?"; Bayesian comparison answers "plausible range true difference, precisely estimated given counts?". edge outgoing transitions source state yields wide credible interval even row-normalised probability looks decisive.

Must use transition method ("relative" / "frequency" aliases). y second object kind x, NULL. x netobject_group y NULL, pairwise comparisons among groups returned. prior Numeric. Dirichlet prior concentration added every cell (default 0.5, Jeffreys prior). Use 1 uniform (Laplace) prior. draws Integer. Number Monte Carlo posterior draws used credible intervals (default 10000). ci Numeric (0, 1). Credible interval mass (default 0.95). mean_threshold Numeric. edge flagged significant absolute posterior mean difference exceeds (default 0.01). bound_threshold Numeric. edge flagged significant credible-interval bound nearest zero exceeds absolute value (default 0.001). Guards differences detectable negligibly small. seed Integer NULL. RNG seed reproducible credible intervals.

Data must wide format transition bootstrap; use convert_sequence_format convert long-format data first. association methods ("cor", "pcor", "glasso", custom estimators), full estimator called resampled rows iteration. transition network contains one sequence, function warns network recommended bootstrap confirmatory testing.

title: Bootstrap a Network Estimate — bootstrap_network
text: x netobject build_network. data, method, params, scaling, threshold, level extracted object. iter Integer. Number bootstrap iterations (default: 1000). ci_level Numeric. Significance level CIs p-values (default: 0.05). inference Character. "stability" (default) tests whether bootstrap replicates fall within multiplicative consistency range around original weight. "threshold" tests whether replicates exceed fixed edge threshold. consistency_range Numeric vector length 2. Multiplicative bounds stability inference (default: c(0.75, 1.25)). edge_threshold Numeric NULL. Fixed threshold inference = "threshold". NULL, defaults 10th percentile absolute original edge weights. seed Integer NULL. RNG seed reproducibility. boundary Character. Comparison rule computing consistency-range p-value. "inclusive" (default, tna-compatible) counts iterations meet bound (\(\le\) / \(\ge\)); "strict" counts iterations strictly outside (\(<\) / \(>\)). ci_method Character. Method edge-weight confidence intervals. "percentile" (default) uses empirical bootstrap quantiles (Efron). "basic" reflects quantiles around observed weight, \((2\hat{\theta} - q_{1-\alpha/2},   2\hat{\theta} - q_{\alpha/2})\) (Davison & Hinkley 1997, eq. 5.6), corrects first-order bootstrap bias can produce bounds outside natural weight range near boundaries (e.g., 0 transition probabilities close 0).

what: Bottleneck Distance Between Persistence Diagrams — bottleneck_distance
title: Bottleneck Distance Between Persistence Diagrams — bottleneck_distance
text: Computes bottleneck distance two persistence diagrams. finite pairs, bottleneck distance $$W_\infty(D_1, D_2) = \inf_{\gamma} \sup_{p \D_1} \|p - \gamma(p)\|_\infty,$$ \(\gamma\) ranges bijections \(D_1 \cup \Delta \D_2 \cup \Delta\) \(\Delta = \{(x,x)\}\) diagonal. point may match point diagram projection onto diagonal cost \(|d - b|/2\). Computed via binary search \(\varepsilon\) plus Kuhn bipartite-matching feasibility check. Essential classes (death = Inf VR mode, death = 0 clique mode) matched one--one within dimension. diagrams different numbers essential classes dimension, bottleneck distance dimension Inf.

FALSE (default) adds nothing; TRUE uses label "Start"; single string uses string label. valid transition methods (relative, frequency, co_occurrence, attention); errors otherwise. end Boundary marker placed single cell sequence's last observed (non-NA) state, explicit terminal state (pure sink: outgoing edges, self-loop – distinct mark_terminal_state, fills trailing NAs absorbing state). FALSE (default) adds nothing; TRUE uses label "End"; single string uses string label. method restriction start. ... Additional arguments passed build_network.

title: Cluster Sequences by Dissimilarity — build_clusters
text: Clusters wide-format sequences using pairwise string dissimilarity either PAM (Partitioning Around Medoids) hierarchical clustering. Supports 9 distance metrics including temporal weighting Hamming distance. stringdist package available, uses C-level distance computation 100-1000x speedup edit distances.

title: Cluster Sequences by Dissimilarity — build_clusters
code: build_clusters(   data,   k,   dissimilarity = "hamming",   method = "pam",   na_syms = c("*", "%"),   weighted = FALSE,   lambda = 1,   seed = NULL,   q = 2L,   p = 0.1,   covariates = NULL,   estimator = c("auto", "firth", "multinom", "chisq"),   ... )

title: Cluster Sequences by Dissimilarity — build_clusters
text: data Input data. Accepts multiple formats: data.frame / matrix Wide-format sequences (rows = sequences, columns = time points, values = state names). netobject network object build_network. Extracts stored sequence data. valid sequence-based methods (relative, frequency, co_occurrence, attention). tna tna model tna package. Decodes integer-encoded sequence data using stored labels. cograph_network cograph network object. Extracts stored sequence data. k Integer. Number clusters (must 2 nrow(data) - 1). dissimilarity Character. Distance metric. One "hamming", "osa" (optimal string alignment), "lv" (Levenshtein), "dl" (Damerau-Levenshtein), "lcs" (longest common subsequence), "qgram", "cosine", "jaccard", "jw" (Jaro-Winkler). Default: "hamming". method Character. Clustering method. "pam" Partitioning Around Medoids, hierarchical method: "ward.D2", "ward.D", "complete", "average", "single", "mcquitty", "median", "centroid". Default: "pam". na_syms Character vector. Symbols treated missing values. Default: c("*", "%"). Missing-value distance rule: symbols converted NA, missing values encoded single comparable sentinel state – pairwise-deleted. Two missing values position match (distance contribution 0); missing value paired observed state mismatches (distance contribution 1 Hamming, etc.). conventional behaviour aligned sequence matrices pairwise deletion change effective length every pair break metric. want pairwise deletion different missing-value semantic, drop recode missing cells passing data . weighted Logical. Apply exponential decay weighting Hamming distance positions? valid dissimilarity = "hamming". Default: FALSE. lambda Numeric. Non-negative decay rate weighted Hamming. Higher values weight earlier positions strongly. Default: 1. seed Integer NULL. Random seed reproducibility. Default: NULL. q Integer. Size q-grams "qgram", "cosine", "jaccard" distances. Default: 2L. p Numeric. Winkler prefix penalty Jaro-Winkler distance. Must 0 0.25. Default: 0.1. covariates Optional. Post-hoc covariate analysis cluster membership. Accepts: string Single column name, e.g. "Age". Resolved x$metadata (x$data) netobject cograph_network input. character vector c("Age", "Gender"), lookup. formula ~ Age + Gender, lookup; supports "Age + Gender" string form . data.frame columns used covariates verbatim; must one row per sequence. NULL covariate analysis (default). netobject cograph_network input, names resolved $metadata first non-state columns $data, typical call looks like build_clusters(net, k = 3, covariates = "session_label") without pre-extracting data.frame. tna input requires data.frame form. Results stored $covariates. estimator Multinomial logit fitter covariate analysis. "auto" (default) inspects cluster x covariate cross-tab falls back "firth" cell fewer 5 observations (quasi-complete separation risk); otherwise uses much faster "multinom". "firth" forces Firth's penalised likelihood via brglm2::brmultinom – bias-reduced finite separation, ~200x slower multinom well-conditioned data. "multinom" forces classical ML via nnet::multinom; warns rare-cell separation produces astronomical ORs degenerate CIs (silent failure). "chisq" runs WeightedCluster-style descriptive tests (chi-square + Cramer's V + standardized adjusted residuals factors; Kruskal-Wallis + eta-squared numerics). ... Unsupported. Supplying unused arguments raises error.

title: Cluster Sequences by Dissimilarity — build_clusters
text: object class "net_clustering" containing: data original input data. k Number clusters. assignments Named integer vector cluster assignments. silhouette Overall average silhouette width. sizes Named integer vector cluster sizes. method Clustering method used. dissimilarity Distance metric used. distance computed dissimilarity matrix (dist object). medoids Integer vector medoid row indices (PAM ; NULL hierarchical methods). seed Seed used (NULL). weighted Logical, whether weighted Hamming used. lambda Lambda value used (0 weighted).

title: Cluster Sequences by Dissimilarity — build_clusters
code: seqs <- data.frame(V1 = c("A","B","C","A","B"), V2 = c("B","C","A","B","A"),                    V3 = c("C","A","B","C","B")) cl <- build_clusters(seqs, k = 2) cl #> Sequence Clustering [pam] #>   Sequences: 5  |  Clusters: 2 #>   Dissimilarity: hamming #>   Quality: silhouette = 0.600 #>  #>   Cluster  N          Mean within-dist  Medoid #>   1        2 (40.0%)  0.000             4 #>   2        3 (60.0%)  2.000             5 # \donttest{ seqs <- data.frame(   V1 = sample(LETTERS[1:3], 20, TRUE), V2 = sample(LETTERS[1:3], 20, TRUE),   V3 = sample(LETTERS[1:3], 20, TRUE), V4 = sample(LETTERS[1:3], 20, TRUE) ) cl <- build_clusters(seqs, k = 2) print(cl) #> Sequence Clustering [pam] #>   Sequences: 20  |  Clusters: 2 #>   Dissimilarity: hamming #>   Quality: silhouette = 0.266 #>  #>   Cluster  N           Mean within-dist  Medoid #>   1        9 (45.0%)   2.389             13 #>   2        11 (55.0%)  2.036             20 summary(cl) #> Sequence Clustering Summary #>   Method:        pam  #>   Dissimilarity: hamming  #>   Silhouette:    0.2659  #>  #> Per-cluster statistics: #>  cluster size mean_within_dist #>        1    9         2.388889 #>        2   11         2.036364 #>   cluster size mean_within_dist #> 1       1    9         2.388889 #> 2       2   11         2.036364 # }

what: GIMME: Group Iterative Multiple Model Estimation — build_gimme
title: GIMME: Group Iterative Multiple Model Estimation — build_gimme
text: Estimates person-specific directed networks intensive longitudinal data using unified Structural Equation Modeling (uSEM) framework. Implements data-driven search identifies: Group-level paths: Directed edges present majority (default 75\ Individual-level paths: Additional edges specific person, found group paths established. Uses lavaan SEM estimation modification indices. Accepts single data frame ID column (CSV directories).

title: GIMME: Group Iterative Multiple Model Estimation — build_gimme
code: build_gimme(   data,   vars,   id,   time = NULL,   ar = TRUE,   standardize = FALSE,   groupcutoff = 0.75,   subcutoff = 0.5,   paths = NULL,   exogenous = NULL,   hybrid = FALSE,   rmsea_cutoff = 0.05,   srmr_cutoff = 0.05,   nnfi_cutoff = 0.95,   cfi_cutoff = 0.95,   n_excellent = 2L,   seed = NULL )

title: GIMME: Group Iterative Multiple Model Estimation — build_gimme
text: data data.frame long format columns person ID, time-varying variables, optionally time/beep column. vars Character vector variable names model. id Character string naming person-ID column. time Character string naming time/order column, NULL. provided, data sorted id time lagging. ar Logical. TRUE (default), autoregressive paths (variable predicting lag 1) included fixed paths. standardize Logical. TRUE (default FALSE), variables standardized per person estimation. Note: returned coefficient network ($coefs, $psi, $temporal_avg, $contemporaneous_avg, $group_paths) unaffected Nestimate extracts standardized lavaan solution (lavInspect(fit, "std")), invariant input scaling. scale-dependent $fit statistics (chisq, aic, bic) change. groupcutoff Numeric 0 1. Proportion individuals path must significant added group level. Default 0.75. subcutoff Numeric. used (reserved future subgrouping). paths Character vector lavaan-syntax paths force model (e.g., "V2~V1lag"). Default NULL. exogenous Character vector variable names treat exogenous. Default NULL. hybrid Logical. TRUE, also searches residual covariances. Default FALSE. rmsea_cutoff Numeric. RMSEA threshold excellent fit (default 0.05). srmr_cutoff Numeric. SRMR threshold excellent fit (default 0.05). nnfi_cutoff Numeric. NNFI/TLI threshold excellent fit (default 0.95). cfi_cutoff Numeric. CFI threshold excellent fit (default 0.95). n_excellent Integer. Number fit indices must excellent stop individual search. Default 2. seed Integer NULL. Random seed reproducibility.

title: GIMME: Group Iterative Multiple Model Estimation — build_gimme
text: S3 object class "net_gimme" containing: temporal p x p matrix group-level temporal (lagged) path counts – entry [,j] = number individuals path j(t-1)->(t). contemporaneous p x p matrix group-level contemporaneous path counts – entry [,j] = number individuals path j(t)->(t). coefs List per-person p x 2p coefficient matrices (rows = endogenous, cols = [lagged, contemporaneous]). psi List per-person residual covariance matrices. fit Data frame per-person fit indices (chisq, df, pvalue, rmsea, srmr, nnfi, cfi, bic, aic, logl, status). path_counts p x 2p matrix: many individuals path. paths List per-person character vectors lavaan path syntax. group_paths Character vector group-level paths found. individual_paths List per-person character vectors individual-level paths (beyond group). syntax List per-person full lavaan syntax strings. labels Character vector variable names. n_subjects Integer. Number individuals. n_obs Integer vector. Time points per individual. config List configuration parameters.

title: GIMME: Group Iterative Multiple Model Estimation — build_gimme
code: # \donttest{ # Create simple panel data (3 subjects, 4 variables, 50 time points). set.seed(42) n_sub <- 3; n_t <- 50; vars <- paste0("V", 1:4) rows <- lapply(seq_len(n_sub), function(i) {   d <- as.data.frame(matrix(rnorm(n_t * 4), ncol = 4))   names(d) <- vars; d$id <- i; d }) panel <- do.call(rbind, rows) res <- build_gimme(panel, vars = vars, id = "id") print(res) #> GIMME Network Analysis #> ------------------------------  #> Subjects:   3  #> Variables:  4  ( V1, V2, V3, V4 ) #> AR paths:   yes  #> Hybrid:     no  #>  #> Group-level paths found: 0  #>  #> Individual-level paths:  mean 0.0, range 0-0 #>  #> Temporal path counts (lagged): #>    V1 V2 V3 V4 #> V1  3  0  0  0 #> V2  0  3  0  0 #> V3  0  0  3  0 #> V4  0  0  0  3 #>  #> Contemporaneous path counts: #>    V1 V2 V3 V4 #> V1  0  0  0  0 #> V2  0  0  0  0 #> V3  0  0  0  0 #> V4  0  0  0  0 # }

title: Build a Higher-Order Network (HON) — build_hon
text: Constructs Higher-Order Network sequential data, faithfully implementing BuildHON algorithm (Xu, Wickramarathne & Chawla, 2016). algorithm detects first-order Markov model insufficient capture sequential dependencies automatically creates higher-order nodes. Uses KL-divergence determine whether extending node's history provides significantly different transition distributions.

title: Build a Higher-Order Network (HON) — build_hon
code: build_hon(   data,   max_order = 5L,   min_freq = 1L,   collapse_repeats = FALSE,   method = "hon+" )

title: Build a Higher-Order Network (HON) — build_hon
text: data One : data.frame: rows trajectories, columns time steps. Trailing NAs stripped. non-NA values coerced character. list: element character (coercible) vector representing one trajectory. tna: tna object sequence data. Numeric state IDs automatically converted label names. netobject: netobject sequence data. max_order Integer. Maximum order HON. Default 5. algorithm may produce lower-order nodes data justify higher orders. min_freq Integer. Minimum frequency transition considered. Transitions observed fewer min_freq times treated zero. Default 1. collapse_repeats Logical. TRUE, adjacent duplicate states within trajectory collapsed analysis. Default FALSE. method Character. Algorithm use: "hon+" (default, parameter-free BuildHON+ lazy observation building MaxDivergence pruning) "hon" (original BuildHON eager observation building).

title: Build a Higher-Order Network (HON) — build_hon
text: S3 object class "net_hon" containing: matrix Weighted adjacency matrix (rows = , cols = ). Rows columns use readable arrow notation (e.g., "-> B"). edges Data frame columns: path (full state sequence, e.g., "-> B -> C"), (context/conditioning states), (predicted next state), count (raw frequency), probability (transition probability), from_order, to_order. nodes data.frame columns id, label, name (one row per HON node; label/name arrow-notation node names). Stored data.frame cograph_network compatibility. n_nodes Number HON nodes. n_edges Number edges. first_order_states Character vector unique original states. max_order_requested max_order parameter used. max_order_observed Highest order actually present. min_freq min_freq parameter used. n_trajectories Number trajectories parsing. directed Logical. Always TRUE.

title: Build a Higher-Order Network (HON) — build_hon
text: Node naming convention: Higher-order nodes use readable arrow notation. first-order node simply "". second-order node representing context "came , now B" "-> B". Third-order: "-> B -> C", etc. Algorithm overview: Count subsequence transitions max_order + 1. Build probability distributions, filtering min_freq. first-order source, recursively test whether extending history (adding context) produces significantly different distribution (via KL-divergence vs. adaptive threshold). Build network accepted rules, rewiring edges higher-order nodes properly connected.

title: Build a Higher-Order Network (HON) — build_hon
text: Xu, J., Wickramarathne, T. L., & Chawla, N. V. (2016). Representing higher-order dependencies networks. Science Advances, 2(5), e1600028. Saebi, M., Xu, J., Kaplan, L. M., Ribeiro, B., & Chawla, N. V. (2020). Efficient modeling higher-order dependencies networks: algorithm application anomaly detection. EPJ Data Science, 9(1), 15.

title: Build a Higher-Order Network (HON) — build_hon
code: seqs <- list(c("A","B","C","D"), c("A","B","C","A"), c("B","C","D","A")) hon <- build_hon(seqs, max_order = 2)  # \donttest{ # From list of trajectories trajs <- list(   c("A", "B", "C", "D", "A"),   c("A", "B", "D", "C", "A"),   c("A", "B", "C", "D", "A") ) hon <- build_hon(trajs, max_order = 3, min_freq = 1) print(hon) #> Higher-Order Network (HON) #>   Nodes:        4 (4 first-order states) #>   Edges:        7 #>   Max order:    1 (requested 3) #>   Min freq:     1 #>   Trajectories: 3 summary(hon) #> Higher-Order Network (HON) Summary #>   Nodes: 4 | Edges: 7 | Trajectories: 3 #>   First-order states: A, B, C, D #>   Max order observed: 1 (requested: 3) #>   Min frequency: 1 #>   Node order distribution: #>     Order 1: 4 nodes #>   from to    weight #> 1    3  1 0.3333333 #> 2    4  1 0.6666667 #> 3    1  2 1.0000000 #> 4    2  3 0.6666667 #> 5    4  3 0.3333333 #> 6    2  4 0.3333333 #> 7    3  4 0.6666667  # From data.frame (rows = trajectories) df <- data.frame(T1 = c("A", "A"), T2 = c("B", "B"),                  T3 = c("C", "D"), T4 = c("D", "C")) hon <- build_hon(df, max_order = 2) # }

what: Detect Path Anomalies via HYPA — build_hypa
title: Detect Path Anomalies via HYPA — build_hypa
text: Constructs k-th order De Bruijn graph sequential trajectory data uses hypergeometric null model detect paths anomalous frequencies. Paths occurring less often expected null model flagged - -represented.

title: Detect Path Anomalies via HYPA — build_hypa
code: build_hypa(   data,   order = 2L,   alpha = 0.05,   min_count = 5L,   p_adjust = "BH",   k = NULL )

title: Detect Path Anomalies via HYPA — build_hypa
text: data data.frame (rows = trajectories), list character vectors, tna object, netobject sequence data. tna/netobject, numeric state IDs automatically converted label names. order Integer scalar integer vector. Order(s) De Bruijn graph (default 2L). order k detects anomalies paths length k. vector supplied, one De Bruijn layer built per order per-order results stored $by_order (named order). orders sorted ascending internally, cograph_network slots ($weights, $edges, $adjacency, $xi, $nodes, $meta) always describe network lowest order produced layer (requested order edges dropped $by_order, $order $k), regardless order vector given; $scores aggregates every built order. alpha Numeric. Significance threshold anomaly classification (default 0.05). Paths HYPA score < alpha -represented; paths score > 1-alpha -represented. min_count Integer. Minimum observed count path classified anomalous (default 5). Paths fewer observations always classified "normal" regardless HYPA score, since rare occurrences unreliable. p_adjust Character. Method multiple testing correction p-values. Default "BH" (Benjamini-Hochberg FDR control). Accepts method p.adjust.methods "none" skip correction. - -representation p-values adjusted separately (two-sided testing). k Deprecated. Former name order; supplied overrides order emits deprecation message. Use order instead.

title: Detect Path Anomalies via HYPA — build_hypa
text: object class c("net_hypa", "cograph_network") components: scores Data frame path, , , observed, expected, ratio, p_value, p_under, p_over, p_adjusted_under, p_adjusted_over, anomaly, order columns (one block rows per requested order). path column shows full state sequence (e.g., "-> B -> C"); context (conditioning states); next state; ratio observed / expected; p_value retained alias p_under, raw lower-tail hypergeometric CDF value; p_over inclusive upper-tail probability P(X >= observed); p_adjusted_under p_adjusted_over corrected p-values - -representation tests respectively. ho_edges Alias scores (orders, arrow notation). Subset scores classified -represented. Subset scores classified -represented. adjacency Weighted adjacency matrix lowest-order De Bruijn graph. weights cograph weight matrix lowest-order graph. xi Fitted propensity matrix lowest-order graph. edges cograph edge data.frame lowest-order graph. by_order Named list per-order result lists. order Integer vector orders actually built (sorted ascending). k Back-compatibility alias order. alpha Significance threshold used. p_adjust Multiple testing correction method used. n_anomalous Number anomalous paths detected (orders). n_over Number -represented paths (orders). n_under Number -represented paths (orders). n_edges Total number edges (orders). nodes data.frame (id, label, name) lowest-order De Bruijn graph nodes (arrow notation). directed Logical. Always TRUE. meta cograph meta list lowest-order graph. node_groups Always NULL.

title: Detect Path Anomalies via HYPA — build_hypa
text: LaRock, T., Nanumyan, V., Scholtes, ., Casiraghi, G., Eliassi-Rad, T., & Schweitzer, F. (2020). HYPA: Efficient Detection Path Anomalies Time Series Data Networks. SDM 2020, 460-468.

title: Detect Path Anomalies via HYPA — build_hypa
code: seqs <- list(c("A","B","C"), c("B","C","A"), c("A","C","B"), c("A","B","C")) hyp <- build_hypa(seqs, order = 2)  # \donttest{ trajs <- list(c("A","B","C"), c("A","B","C"), c("A","B","C"),               c("A","B","D"), c("C","B","D"), c("C","B","A")) h <- build_hypa(trajs, order = 2) print(h) #> HYPA: Path Anomaly Detection #>   Order(s):     2 #>   Edges:        4 #>   Anomalous:    0 (alpha=0.05, p_adjust=BH) #>     Over-repr:  0 #>     Under-repr: 0 # }

what: Higher-order hypergraph from a network's clique structure — build_hypergraph
title: Higher-order hypergraph from a network's clique structure — build_hypergraph
text: Takes network produces hypergraph promoting k-cliques (k >= 3) k-hyperedges. k-clique independently included k-hyperedge probability p. Optionally retains underlying pairwise edges 2-hyperedges. Foundation higher-order analyses.

title: Higher-order hypergraph from a network's clique structure — build_hypergraph
code: build_hypergraph(   net,   p = 1,   method = c("clique", "vr", "rips"),   include_pairwise = TRUE,   max_size = 3L,   threshold = 0,   seed = NULL )  # S3 method for class 'net_hypergraph' print(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'net_hypergraph' summary(object, ...)

title: Higher-order hypergraph from a network's clique structure — build_hypergraph
text: net netobject, cograph_network, simplicial_complex, numeric adjacency / weight matrix. Directed inputs symmetrised underlying clique enumerator. p Probability [0, 1] k-clique k >= 3 becomes k-hyperedge. Default 1 (deterministic — every found clique included). method Hyperedge enumeration. "clique" (default) promotes k-cliques binarised adjacency k-hyperedges. metric Vietoris-Rips construction implemented; "vr" / "rips" accepted match.arg raise error rather silently aliasing "clique". include_pairwise Logical. Include 2-edges input network 2-hyperedges. Default TRUE. Set FALSE "fully higher-order" hypergraph containing k-hyperedges k >= 3. max_size Integer >= 2. Maximum hyperedge size extract. Default 3L (triangles ). 4L also includes 4-cliques 4-hyperedges, etc. threshold Numeric. Edge weight cutoff used binarise adjacency clique enumeration. Default 0 (non-zero weight edge). seed Optional integer reproducible Bernoulli sampling 0 < p < 1. x net_hypergraph object (print). ... Additional arguments (ignored). object net_hypergraph object (summary).

title: Higher-order hypergraph from a network's clique structure — build_hypergraph
text: net_hypergraph object: list components hyperedges List integer vectors. entry hyperedge given sorted node indices spans. incidence Numeric matrix size n_nodes x n_hyperedges. incidence[, j] = 1 iff node belongs hyperedge j. Row names node names; column names h1, h2, ... nodes Character vector node names. n_nodes, n_hyperedges Scalar counts. size_distribution Named integer vector: number hyperedges size, named size_2, size_3, ... params Recorded call parameters: method, p, include_pairwise, max_size, threshold, seed. input x invisibly. input object invisibly.

title: Higher-order hypergraph from a network's clique structure — build_hypergraph
text: construction follows Burgio, Matamalas, Gomez & Arenas (2020) simplicial / hypergraph contagion. k-clique k >= 3 found underlying graph (via build_simplicial()), independent Bernoulli(p) trial decides whether clique becomes k-hyperedge. Underlying pairwise edges always retained include_pairwise = TRUE, resulting hypergraph contains original 2-edge structure sampled higher-order interactions. limits: p = 0 include_pairwise = TRUE reproduces input pairwise network hypergraph size-2 edges. p = 1 include_pairwise = FALSE returns fully higher-order hypergraph containing k-hyperedges (k >= 3) found network's clique complex.

title: Higher-order hypergraph from a network's clique structure — build_hypergraph
text: Burgio, G., Matamalas, J. T., Gomez, S., & Arenas, . (2020). Evolution cooperation presence higher-order interactions: networks hypergraphs. Entropy 22(7), 744. doi:10.3390/e22070744

title: Higher-order hypergraph from a network's clique structure — build_hypergraph
code: set.seed(1) n <- 8 adj <- matrix(stats::rbinom(n * n, 1, 0.5), n, n) diag(adj) <- 0 adj <- (adj + t(adj)) > 0 rownames(adj) <- colnames(adj) <- LETTERS[seq_len(n)] hg <- build_hypergraph(adj, p = 1, max_size = 3L) print(hg) #> Hypergraph: 8 nodes, 51 hyperedges #> Size distribution: #>   size_2   : 23 #>   size_3   : 28 #> Method: clique, p = 1.00, include_pairwise = TRUE, max_size = 3 summary(hg) #> Hypergraph summary #>   Nodes:         8 #>   Hyperedges:    51 #>   Mean size:     2.55 #>   Max size:      3 #>   node degree #> 1    A     17 #> 2    B     17 #> 3    C     23 #> 4    D     17 #> 5    E     17 #> 6    F     13 #> 7    G     13 #> 8    H     13

what: Build MCML from Raw Transition Data — build_mcml
title: Build MCML from Raw Transition Data — build_mcml
text: Builds Multi-Cluster Multi-Level (MCML) model raw transition data (edge lists sequences) recoding node labels cluster labels counting actual transitions. Unlike cluster_summary aggregates pre-computed weight matrix, function works original transition data produce TRUE Markov chain cluster states.

title: Build MCML from Raw Transition Data — build_mcml
code: build_mcml(   x,   clusters = NULL,   method = c("sum", "mean", "median", "max", "min", "density", "geomean"),   type = c("tna", "frequency", "cooccurrence", "raw"),   directed = TRUE,   compute_within = TRUE,   actor = NULL,   action = NULL,   time = NULL,   order = NULL,   session = NULL,   time_threshold = 900,   labels = NULL )

title: Build MCML from Raw Transition Data — build_mcml
text: x Input data. Accepts multiple formats: data.frame /columns Edge list. Columns named /source/src/v1/node1//target/tgt/v2/node2/j auto-detected. Optional weight column (weight/w/value/strength). data.frame without /columns Sequence data. row sequence, columns time steps. Consecutive pairs (t, t+1) become transitions. tna object x$data non-NULL, uses sequence path raw data. Otherwise falls back cluster_summary. netobject x$data non-NULL, detects edge list vs sequence data. Otherwise falls back cluster_summary. cluster_summary Returns -. square numeric matrix Falls back cluster_summary. non-square character matrix Treated sequence data. clusters Cluster/group assignments. Accepts: named list Direct mapping. List names = cluster names, values = character vectors node labels. Example: list(= c("N1","N2"), B = c("N3","N4")) data.frame data frame first column contains node names second column contains group/cluster names. Example: data.frame(node = c("N1","N2","N3"), group = c("","","B")) membership vector Character numeric vector. Node names extracted data. Example: c("","","B","B") column name string edge list data.frames, name column containing cluster labels. mapping built unique (node, group) pairs columns. Limitation: mode assigns row's group label endpoints, makes sense edge lists source target nodes always share group (within-group edges ). general edge lists single node may source rows target others, source target belong different groups, pass explicit named list (list(G1 = c("N1","N2"), ...)) two-column data frame data.frame(node, group) instead. NULL Auto-detect netobject$nodes $node_groups (logic cluster_summary). method Aggregation method combining edge weights: "sum", "mean", "median", "max", "min", "density", "geomean". Default "sum". raw sequence/event-log inputs function counting observed transitions, "sum" interpretation preserves count semantics – methods useful aggregating weighted edge lists pre-existing weight matrices, row already represents measurement rather single observation. type Post-processing aggregated count matrix. One : "tna" (default) Row-normalize row sums 1 (first-order Markov transition probabilities). "raw" Return un-normalized count matrix. "frequency" Explicit alias "raw" – identical raw count construction (kept synonym callers using frequency-network terminology). "cooccurrence" Symmetrize matrix (undirected co-occurrence). "semi_markov" accepted: package implement semi-Markov / holding-time construction, passing errors rather silently aliasing "tna". directed Logical. TRUE (default), treat transitions directed. FALSE, symmetrize sequence- edge-derived weights returning raw/frequency weights row-normalizing transition probabilities. compute_within Logical. Compute within-cluster matrices? Default TRUE. actor, action, time, order, session, time_threshold Long-format event-log shortcut. action supplied data.frame input, data passed prepare() derive wide sequence, routed existing sequence path. Behaves identically prepare(...) |> build_network() |> build_mcml(). labels Optional name -> label remap applied within-cluster nodes (macro layer left untouched labels cluster names). Accepts 2-column data.frame (name, label), named character vector c(name = "label"), named list. Unmapped names pass unchanged.

what: Multi-Cluster Multi-Level Aggregation for Psychometric Networks — build_mcml_pc
title: Multi-Cluster Multi-Level Aggregation for Psychometric Networks — build_mcml_pc
text: Experimental. Aggregates node-level psychometric network (correlation, partial correlation, EBICglasso) cluster-level macro network plus per-cluster within networks — MCML view build_mcml provides transition networks, adapted statistics undirected association networks. API exact aggregation formulas may change releases. Unlike transition counts, partial correlations aggregate arithmetic: submatrix pcor matrix pcor network subsystem (conditioning set changes), averaging pcor entries across blocks descriptive . five aggregation methods therefore explicitly different statuses: "average" (descriptive) Macro edge –B = mean signed node-level weights members members B; macro diagonal = mean within-block -diagonal weight. Needs weight matrix (works data-less netobjects). Caveat: signed averaging can cancel opposite-sign edges; interpret net average association, connectivity strength. "composite" (re-estimated) Per-observation cluster scores = (optionally standardized) mean member variables; chosen method re-fit k composite columns, macro network genuine correlation / pcor / EBICglasso network among clusters. Requires raw data. "loadings" (re-estimated, connectivity-weighted) "composite", member variables weighted within-cluster connection strength node-level network (absolute summed weight members cluster, normalized sum 1 per cluster). Nodes anchor cluster contribute composite. Nestimate's weighting — related spirit network loadings (Christensen & Golino 2021) reimplementation EGA-family estimator. Requires raw data. "rv" (descriptive, multivariate) Macro edge –B = Escoufier's RV coefficient member blocks — matrix correlation [0, 1] computed block covariance structure. composites formed estimator re-fit, nothing lost averaging; signs represented. Requires raw data. "canonical" (descriptive, multivariate) Macro edge –B = first canonical correlation member blocks: strongest linear relationship weighting 's items can weighting B's items — upper bound composite methods can recover. Requires raw data.

title: Multi-Cluster Multi-Level Aggregation for Psychometric Networks — build_mcml_pc
text: x netobject estimated undirected association method (cor, pcor, glasso; aliases accepted) — $data $method reused — numeric data.frame raw observations (method decides estimator). clusters Cluster membership: named list node-label vectors (names = cluster labels), vector cluster labels named node. Every node must assigned exactly one cluster. aggregation Character. "average", "composite", "loadings", "rv", "canonical" (see Description). Default "average". method Character. Network estimator re-estimation paths data.frame input: "pcor" (default), "glasso", "cor" — vocabulary build_network. Ignored (netobject's method used instead) x netobject method given. within Character. "reestimate" (default) "subnetwork" (see Details). weighting Character. items weighted inside cluster composite (aggregation = "composite" ): "equal" (default) 1/m per item — scale scored. "strength" Mean absolute connection -cluster members node-level network (aggregation = "loadings" selects). "eigen" Leading-eigenvector weights within-cluster block node-level network — like strength giving extra weight items connected well-connected items. "pca" First principal component member items' correlation matrix — data-statistical weighting, blind estimated network. "factor" Standardized loadings one-factor model per cluster — classical latent-variable weighting. extraction method chosen fa_method correlation input respects cor_method (polychoric factor analysis ordinal items one call). Clusters fewer 3 items (non-converging fits) fall back "pca" warning. "closeness", "betweenness" Within-cluster closeness / betweenness centrality item node-level network block (absolute weights). Betweenness can -zero densely connected clusters; equal weights used , warning. "expected_influence" Mean signed connection -cluster members (Robinaugh et al. 2016) — like strength, opposite-sign connections subtract, negative expected influence marks reverse-keyed items. "specificity" misfit margin: -cluster strength minus strongest cross-cluster strength, floored 0. Items belong much another cluster contribute nothing composite — weighting twin misfit diagnostic. "item_total" Corrected item-total correlation: item mean (standardized) members — classical scale-construction weighting. Custom weightings: weighting also accepts named numeric vector (one entry per item; absolute values normalized within cluster, signs flip items signed = TRUE) function function(W_block, data_block, nodes) returning one numeric weight per item, evaluated per cluster. Network-based data-based weightings answer different questions; comparing informative — divergence means network's view cluster differs latent-variable view. Schemes inherently non-negative weights (equal, closeness, betweenness, specificity) keep eigenvector-based item signs; sign-carrying schemes (eigen, pca, factor, expected_influence, item_total, custom) use . scale Logical. Standardize member variables forming composites (default TRUE). Ignored "average", "rv", "canonical". cor_method Character. Correlation type estimation raw data: "pearson" (default), "spearman", "polychoric" (needs lavaan; see Details). signed Logical. Flip reverse-keyed items composites (default TRUE; see Details). id_col Character vector NULL. Identifier column(s) drop data.frame input analysis (e.g., rid/actor columns produced convert_sequence_format(format = "frequency"), whose output otherwise feeds function directly per-actor behavior profiles). convention association estimators. fa_method Character. Extraction method weighting = "factor": "ml" (default) Maximum likelihood (stats::factanal cor_method-consistent correlation matrix). "paf" Iterated principal axis factoring (SMC start, communalities iterated reduced correlation matrix). "minres" Minimum residual / unweighted least squares (uniquenesses optimized minimize squared -diagonal residuals). "cfa" One-factor confirmatory model lavaan (standardized loadings); cor_method = "polychoric" items declared ordered, giving categorical (DWLS) factor model. well-behaved unidimensional clusters four agree closely; divergence indicates Heywood-prone non-unidimensional clusters. ... arguments forwarded directly fa_method backend, exactly backend spells (requires weighting = "factor"): lavaan arguments "cfa" (estimator = "WLSMV", missing = "fiml", se = "robust", ...), stats::factanal() arguments "ml", max_iter / tol "paf". Arguments managed internally (model, data, covmat, n.obs, factors) ignored warning — model always one-factor model per cluster, composite needs exactly one weight per item.

text: object class "mcml_pc" containing: macro Cluster-level netobject (k x k, undirected). clusters Named list within-cluster netobjects (NULL singleton clusters). cluster_members Named list member node labels. loadings Tidy item-diagnostic data frame (one row per node): node, cluster, loading (signed -cluster), weight, sign, max_cross, cross_cluster, misfit. NULL node-level network available. node_network node-level netobject aggregation based (kept diagnostics loading_stability). data raw data (data.frame) available, else NULL. meta List: aggregation, method, within, scale, cor_method, signed, n_nodes, n_clusters, cluster_sizes, n_misfit, n_flipped, directed = FALSE, source = "pc", experimental = TRUE.

title: Multi-Cluster Multi-Level Aggregation for Psychometric Networks — build_mcml_pc
text: Item diagnostics. Whenever raw data node-level network available, every item's connection strength every cluster computed. $loadings table reports, per item: signed -cluster loading, composite weight, strongest cross-cluster loading, misfit flag set cross-cluster loading exceeds -cluster loading — evidence item assigned wrong cluster. Misfit items trigger warning; every aggregation silently inherits bad membership, fix assignment rather ignoring flag. Reverse-keyed items. signed = TRUE (default), items whose summed within-cluster association negative flipped (standardized values enter composites weight sign -1), reverse-keyed item reinforces cluster composite instead cancelling . Flips reported sign column via warning. Missing data. Composites per-row weighted means observed members (weights renormalized per row); rows observed member yield NA dropped estimator message. Node-level estimation applies estimators' complete-case handling. Ordinal items. cor_method = "polychoric" (requires lavaan package) estimates node-level within-cluster networks polychoric correlations — appropriate Likert items. Composites continuous sums, macro re-estimation uses Pearson correlations composites regardless. Within-cluster networks follow within: "reestimate" (default) re-fits estimator member columns alone — honest conditional structure subsystem; "subnetwork" slices node-level weight matrix descriptive (pcor/glasso retains conditioning --cluster nodes). Modes without raw data force "subnetwork". Singleton clusters get within network (NULL). Uncertainty. composite/loadings macro network full netobject carrying composite data, bootstrap_network(fit$macro) (edge-weight CIs) vertex_bootstrap(fit$macro) (network-level CIs) work directly; vertex_compare(fit1$macro, fit2$macro) compares two groups. loading_stability quantifies stable composite weights case resampling. constituent networks undirected (meta$directed = FALSE), renderers auto-detect directedness (e.g. cograph::plot_mcml()) draw result without arrowheads.

title: Multi-Cluster Multi-Level Aggregation for Psychometric Networks — build_mcml_pc
code: set.seed(1) n <- 200 sigma <- matrix(0.15, 6, 6) sigma[1:3, 1:3] <- 0.5 sigma[4:6, 4:6] <- 0.5 diag(sigma) <- 1 z <- matrix(rnorm(n * 6), n, 6) %*% chol(sigma) df <- as.data.frame(z) names(df) <- c("a1", "a2", "a3", "b1", "b2", "b3") clusters <- list(A = c("a1", "a2", "a3"), B = c("b1", "b2", "b3"))  fit <- build_mcml_pc(df, clusters, aggregation = "composite",                      method = "cor") fit$macro$weights #>          A        B #> A 0.000000 0.235001 #> B 0.235001 0.000000 fit$loadings #>   node cluster   loading    weight sign max_cross cross_cluster misfit #> 1   a1       A 0.4738902 0.3333333    1 0.1671718             B  FALSE #> 2   a2       A 0.4537919 0.3333333    1 0.1602877             B  FALSE #> 3   a3       A 0.4736153 0.3333333    1 0.1400550             B  FALSE #> 4   b1       B 0.5368416 0.3333333    1 0.1618540             A  FALSE #> 5   b2       B 0.5156674 0.3333333    1 0.1811768             A  FALSE #> 6   b3       B 0.5168036 0.3333333    1 0.1244837             A  FALSE

what: Build a Multilevel Vector Autoregression (mlVAR) network — build_mlvar
title: Build a Multilevel Vector Autoregression (mlVAR) network — build_mlvar
text: Estimates three networks ESM/EMA panel data, matching mlVAR::mlVAR() estimator = "lmer", temporal = "fixed", contemporaneous = "fixed" machine precision: (1) directed temporal network fixed-effect lagged regression coefficients, (2) undirected contemporaneous network partial correlations among residuals, (3) undirected -subjects network partial correlations derived person-mean fixed effects. #' @details algorithm follows mlVAR's lmer pipeline exactly: Drop rows NA id/day/beep optionally grand-mean standardize variable. Expand per-(id, day) beep grid right-join original values, producing augmented panel (augData). Add within-person lagged predictors (L1_*) person-mean predictors (PM_*). outcome variable fit lmer(y ~ within + -except--PM + (1 | id)) REML = FALSE. Collect fixed-effect temporal matrix B, -effect matrix Gamma, random-intercept SDs (mu_SD), lmer residual SDs. Contemporaneous network: cor2pcor(D %*% cov2cor(cor(resid)) %*% D). -subjects network: cor2pcor(pseudoinverse(forcePositive(D (- Gamma)))). Validated machine precision (max_diff < 1e-10) mlVAR::mlVAR() 25 real ESM datasets openesm 20 simulated configurations (seeds 201-220). See tmp/mlvar_equivalence_real20.R tmp/mlvar_equivalence_20seeds.R.

title: Build a Multilevel Vector Autoregression (mlVAR) network — build_mlvar
code: build_mlvar(   data,   vars,   id,   day = NULL,   beep = NULL,   lag = 1L,   standardize = FALSE )

NULL, row position within (id, day) used. lag Integer. lag order (default 1). standardize Logical. TRUE, variable grand-mean centered divided pooled SD augmentation. Default FALSE, matching mlVAR::mlVAR(scale = FALSE) — setting numerical equivalence validated.

title: Build a Multilevel Vector Autoregression (mlVAR) network — build_mlvar
text: dual-class c("net_mlvar", "netobject_group") object — named list three full netobjects, one per network, plus model-level metadata stored attributes. element standard c("netobject", "cograph_network") weight-matrix wrapper (raw $data), print(), summary(), coefs(), cograph::splot(fit$temporal) work directly. three constituents matrix-wrapped carry underlying panel data, data-resampling verbs bootstrap_network() (reliability/stability) iterate — extract single constituent rebuild via build_network() need . Structure: fit$temporal Directed netobject d x d matrix fixed-effect lagged coefficients. $weights[, j] effect variable j t-lag variable t. method = "mlvar_temporal", directed = TRUE. fit$contemporaneous Undirected netobject d x d partial-correlation network within-person lmer residuals. method = "mlvar_contemporaneous", directed = FALSE. fit$Undirected netobject d x d partial-correlation network person means, derived D (- Gamma). method = "mlvar_between", directed = FALSE. attr(fit, "coefs") / coefs() Tidy data.frame one row per (outcome, predictor) pair columns outcome, predictor, beta, se, t, p, ci_lower, ci_upper, significant. Filter, sort, plot base R tidyverse. Retrieve coefs(fit). attr(fit, "n_obs") Number rows augmented panel na.omit. attr(fit, "n_subjects") Number unique subjects remaining. attr(fit, "lag") Lag order used. attr(fit, "standardize") Logical; whether pre-augmentation standardization applied.
