Hypergraph eigenvector centralities — hypergraph_centrality
text: Computes one eigenvector-style centralities net_hypergraph: clique-motif (CEC), Z-eigenvector (ZEC), H-eigenvector (HEC). variant captures influence differently — CEC flattens group structure via clique expansion, ZEC HEC propagate higher-order groups directly.

title: Hypergraph eigenvector centralities — hypergraph_centrality
code: hypergraph_centrality(   hg,   type = c("clique", "Z", "H"),   max_iter = 1000L,   tol = 1e-08,   normalize = TRUE )

title: Hypergraph eigenvector centralities — hypergraph_centrality
text: hg net_hypergraph (build_hypergraph() bipartite_groups()). type Character vector, subset c("clique", "Z", "H"). Default computes three. max_iter Maximum number power-iteration steps. Default 1000. tol Convergence tolerance L1 change successive iterates. Default 1e-8. normalize Logical. TRUE (default), returned centrality vector L2-normalized unit norm (compatible igraph::eigen_centrality()'s scale type "clique").

title: Hypergraph eigenvector centralities — hypergraph_centrality
text: named list; one component per requested type. component named numeric vector length hg$n_nodes.

title: Hypergraph eigenvector centralities — hypergraph_centrality
text:
Clique-motif eigenvector centrality (CEC): forms clique-expanded pairwise graph \(W\) \(W_{ij} = |\{e : , j \e\}|\) returns leading eigenvector \(W\). Equivalent running igraph::eigen_centrality() clique_expansion() output. Z-eigenvector centrality (ZEC): solves linear eigen-equation hyperedge tensor, $$\lambda\, x_i \;=\; \sum_{e 
i }\; \prod_{j \e,\; j 
eq } x_j,$$ via power iteration. Works hypergraphs mixed edge sizes. H-eigenvector centrality (HEC): solves power-k-1 eigen-equation, $$\lambda\, x_i^{k-1} \;=\; \sum_{e 
i }\; \prod_{j \e,\; j 
eq } x_j.$$ uniform hypergraphs (hyperedges size \(k\)), equivalent normalizing ZEC update geometric-mean exponent \(1/(k-1)\). mixed sizes, effective exponent taken largest hyperedge; expect slightly different rankings ZEC mixed case.

title: Hypergraph eigenvector centralities — hypergraph_centrality
text: "clique" (CEC) variant validated igraph::eigen_centrality (cosine ~ 1). "Z" "H" variants (experimental) — validated clean-room list-based tensor power iteration (operator, different loop structure); R package exposes tensor eigenvectors primitive independent comparison.

title: Hypergraph eigenvector centralities — hypergraph_centrality
text: Benson, . R. (2019). Three hypergraph eigenvector centralities. SIAM Journal Mathematics Data Science 1(2), 293-312. arXiv:1807.09644.

title: Hypergraph eigenvector centralities — hypergraph_centrality
code: df <- data.frame(   player  = c("A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "D", "C", "D", "E"),   session = c("S1", "S1", "S1", "S2", "S2", "S3", "S3", "S3", "S3") ) hg <- bipartite_groups(df, "player", "session") cent <- hypergraph_centrality(hg) # Compare rankings across the three variants do.call(cbind, cent) #>      clique            Z         H #> A 0.5345225 6.666667e-01 0.6147176 #> B 0.5345225 6.666667e-01 0.6147176 #> C 0.5345225 3.333333e-01 0.4216259 #> D 0.2672612 3.983626e-09 0.1823130 #> E 0.2672612 3.983626e-09 0.1823130

what: Structural measures for a hypergraph — hypergraph_measures
title: Structural measures for a hypergraph — hypergraph_measures
text: Computes comprehensive structural-statistics suite net_hypergraph: node-level, hyperedge-level, global measures. measures derived BLAS calls incidence matrix.

title: Structural measures for a hypergraph — hypergraph_measures
code: hypergraph_measures(hg)  # S3 method for class 'hypergraph_measures' print(x, ...)

title: Structural measures for a hypergraph — hypergraph_measures
text: hg net_hypergraph (build_hypergraph() bipartite_groups()). x hypergraph_measures object. ... Additional arguments (ignored).

title: Structural measures for a hypergraph — hypergraph_measures
text: object class hypergraph_measures (named list) components: Node-level (length n_nodes)  hyperdegree Number hyperedges containing node. node_strength Total participation: node \(\), \(\sum_{e \ni } |e|\). node many large hyperedges high strength. max_edge_size Size largest hyperedge containing node. co_degree n_nodes x n_nodes matrix: co_degree[, j] = |{e : , j e}| (number hyperedges co-containing nodes \(\) \(j\)). Diagonal zero. Hyperedge-level (length n_hyperedges m x m)  edge_sizes Hyperedge sizes \(|e|\). edge_pairwise_overlap m x m matrix: |e_i intersect e_j|. Diagonal zero. overlap_coefficient m x m: |e_i e_j| / min(|e_i|, |e_j|). Measures much smaller hyperedge contained larger. jaccard m x m: symmetric overlap index |e_i e_j| / |e_i union e_j|. Global (scalars)  density k-uniform hypergraphs: m / choose(n, k). mixed sizes: sum(|e|) / (n * m) (mean fraction nodes per hyperedge). avg_edge_size Mean edge_sizes. size_distribution Tabulation hyperedge sizes (passed hg). intersection_profile Distribution pairwise hyperedge intersection sizes — useful spotting whether hyperedges overlap mostly trivially share substantial cores (et al. 2020). pairwise_participation Fraction node pairs co-appearing least one hyperedge. n_nodes, n_hyperedges Convenience scalars. input x invisibly.

title: Structural measures for a hypergraph — hypergraph_measures
text: measures computed via standard matrix operations binary incidence \(B = (b_{ij})\) \(b_{ij} = 1\) iff node \(\) hyperedge \(j\): hyperdegree = rowSums(B), edge_sizes = colSums(B) co_degree = tcrossprod(B) (zero diagonal) edge_pairwise_overlap = crossprod(B) (zero diagonal) overlap_coefficient[, j] = overlap[, j] / min(edge_sizes[], edge_sizes[j]) jaccard[, j] = overlap[, j] / (edge_sizes[] + edge_sizes[j] - overlap[, j]) Empty hypergraph (n_hyperedges == 0) returns trivial zeros empty matrices.

title: Structural measures for a hypergraph — hypergraph_measures
text: Lee, G., Choe, M., & Shin, K. (2024). survey hypergraph representation, learning mining. Data Mining & Knowledge Discovery 37, 1-39. , M. T., Yoon, S., Hooi, B., & Shin, K. (2020). Structural patterns generative models real-world hypergraphs. arXiv:2006.07060.

title: Structural measures for a hypergraph — hypergraph_measures
code: df <- data.frame(   player  = c("A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "D", "C", "D"),   session = c("S1", "S1", "S1", "S2", "S2", "S3", "S3", "S3") ) hg <- bipartite_groups(df, "player", "session") m  <- hypergraph_measures(hg) print(m) #> Hypergraph measures: 4 nodes, 3 hyperedges #>   Density:                0.5833 #>   Mean edge size:         2.33 #>   Pairwise participation: 0.6667 #>  #>   Hyperdegree:    min=1  med=2.0  max=2 #>   Node strength:  min=2.0  med=5.0  max=5.0 #>   Edge sizes:     min=2  med=2.0  max=3 #>  #> Intersection profile (pair counts by overlap size): #>   overlap_0   : 1 #>   overlap_1   : 1 #>   overlap_2   : 1 m$hyperdegree         # how many sessions each player joined #> A B C D  #> 2 2 2 1  m$co_degree           # pairwise co-membership counts #>   A B C D #> A 0 2 1 0 #> B 2 0 1 0 #> C 1 1 0 1 #> D 0 0 1 0 m$jaccard             # symmetric overlap between sessions #>           S1        S2   S3 #> S1 0.0000000 0.6666667 0.25 #> S2 0.6666667 0.0000000 0.00 #> S3 0.2500000 0.0000000 0.00

title: Composite-Weight Stability Under Case Resampling — loading_stability
text: Experimental. Bootstraps item weights build_mcml_pc fit: rows raw data resampled, node-level network re-estimated time, connectivity-based composite weights recomputed. Wide intervals mean weighting (therefore "loadings" macro network) -interpreted.

title: Composite-Weight Stability Under Case Resampling — loading_stability
code: loading_stability(x, iter = 200L, ci_level = 0.05, seed = NULL)

title: Composite-Weight Stability Under Case Resampling — loading_stability
text: x mcml_pc object carries raw data. iter Integer. Bootstrap replicates (default 200; node-level re-estimation makes heavier plain bootstrap). ci_level Numeric. Significance level percentile CIs (default 0.05). seed Integer NULL. RNG seed.

title: Composite-Weight Stability Under Case Resampling — loading_stability
text: object class "pc_loading_stability": list summary (tidy data frame: node, cluster, weight, boot_mean, boot_sd, ci_lower, ci_upper, sign_flips — proportion replicates item's sign differed observed one), boot_weights (iter x n_nodes matrix), iter, ci_level. print plot methods.

title: Composite-Weight Stability Under Case Resampling — loading_stability
code: # \donttest{ set.seed(1) df <- as.data.frame(matrix(rnorm(600), 100, 6)) names(df) <- c("a1", "a2", "a3", "b1", "b2", "b3") cl <- list(A = c("a1", "a2", "a3"), B = c("b1", "b2", "b3")) fit <- build_mcml_pc(df, cl, aggregation = "loadings",                      estimator = "cor") #> Error: 'estimator' in ... is the lavaan estimator (e.g. "WLSMV") and applies only to fa_method = "cfa". The network estimator is selected by 'method'. ls <- loading_stability(fit, iter = 50, seed = 1) #> Error: object 'fit' not found ls$summary #> Error in ls$summary: object of type 'closure' is not subsettable # }

Default: "Time". action_col Character. Name column containing actions/states. Default: "Action". time_prefix Character. Prefix time point columns output. Default: "V". fill_na Logical. what: Magnitude difference between the frequency and probability views — magnitude_difference
title: Magnitude difference between the frequency and probability views — magnitude_difference
text: Quantifies, per edge, much row-normalization moves transition network two natural summaries: raw transition counts (frequency / FTNA, build_network(method = "frequency")) row-conditional probabilities (TNA, build_network(method = "relative")). two matrices rank edges differently — edge large counts can modest probability, rare-source edge can dominate row probability. per-edge discrepancy common scale magnitude difference.

title: Magnitude difference between the frequency and probability views — magnitude_difference
code: magnitude_difference(   data,   actor = "Actor",   action = "Action",   time = NULL,   metric = c("abs_diff", "chord_dist", "atanh_diff", "geom_norm_diff", "cv_inflation"),   scale = c("tna_range", "rank_minmax", "minmax", "none"),   format = c("auto", "long", "wide") )  # S3 method for class 'magnitude_difference' print(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'magnitude_difference' plot(x, type = c("stacked", "circular"), min_show = 0.01, title = NULL, ...)

title: Magnitude difference between the frequency and probability views — magnitude_difference
text: data Long- wide-format event log (data.frame). actor, action, time Column names data (long format). time may NULL. metric Discrepancy metric. One "abs_diff" (default; absolute difference, simplest robust), "chord_dist" (chord distance unit sphere), "atanh_diff" (Fisher z-style bounded scale), "geom_norm_diff" (geometric-mean normalized; amplifies small edges), "cv_inflation" (per-vector SD-standardized absolute difference). scale two weight matrices placed common scale differencing. "tna_range" (default) rescales FTNA linearly TNA's [min, max] range leaves TNA untouched, difference TNA probability units. "rank_minmax" converts matrix's values ranks scaled [0, 1] (ordinal). "minmax" scales matrix's raw values [0, 1] separately (asymmetric — TNA's max FTNA's max map value despite differing native ranges). "none" uses raw weights. format Input format passed build_network(); "auto" (default) treats data wide action column. x magnitude_difference object. ... Passed plotting helpers (ignored print). type Plot style, "stacked" (default) "circular". min_show type = "circular", drop edges whose magnitude fraction maximum. title Plot title. NULL generates one metric scale.

title: Magnitude difference between the frequency and probability views — magnitude_difference
text: object class "magnitude_difference": list $edges (per-edge data.frame columns , , ftna, tna, signed = tna - ftna, value = chosen metric), $metric, $scale, $weights_ftna, $weights_tna, $states. print invisibly returns x. plot returns ggplot object.

title: Magnitude difference between the frequency and probability views — magnitude_difference
text: print(magnitude_difference): Print compact summary per-edge magnitude-difference distribution. plot(magnitude_difference): Plot per-edge magnitude difference polar portrait. type = "stacked" (default) draws one sector per -state stacked wedges (grey base = shared value, colored tip = magnitude difference); type = "circular" draws chord-style diagram signed differences diverging blue-orange scale.

title: Magnitude difference between the frequency and probability views — magnitude_difference
code: data(group_regulation_long, package = "Nestimate") fit <- magnitude_difference(group_regulation_long,                             actor = "Actor", action = "Action",                             time = "Time") print(fit) #> magnitude_difference object #>   metric: abs_diff   scale: tna_range  #>   states (9):  adapt, cohesion, consensus, coregulate, discuss, emotion, monitor, plan, synthesis #>   edges: 81  #>   magnitude-difference summary (abs_diff): #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #> 0.00000 0.01143 0.03002 0.06035 0.06894 0.42910  # Edges most promoted by row-normalization (rare-source transitions): head(fit$edges[order(-fit$edges$signed), c("from", "to", "ftna", "tna")]) #>         from        to       ftna       tna #> 19     adapt consensus 0.04830269 0.4774067 #> 27 synthesis consensus 0.06042805 0.4662577 #> 20  cohesion consensus 0.16776736 0.4979351 #> 43   monitor   discuss 0.10694175 0.3754361 #> 10     adapt  cohesion 0.02762993 0.2730845 #> 9  synthesis     adapt 0.03041280 0.2346626 # \donttest{ plot(fit)                       # stacked polar portrait  plot(fit, type = "circular")    # chord-style diagram  # }

what: Mark leading-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_first_state
title: Mark leading-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_first_state
text: Mirror mark_terminal_state() left-censored sequence data. Replaces every cell row's first observed state label given state. resulting chain structurally recurrent "Start" state everyone enters — useful cohort-entry analyses students join different time points want uniform pre-observation marker.

title: Mark leading-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_first_state
code: mark_first_state(data, state = "Start", cols = NULL)

title: Mark leading-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_first_state
text: data wide-format matrix data.frame (rows = actors, cols = time steps) state labels NA missing observations. state Character. Label insert leading-NA cells. Default "Start". cols Optional state-column names; otherwise columns.

title: Mark leading-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_first_state
text: data.frame shape data leading NAs filled state.

title: Mark leading-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_first_state
text: Unlike mark_terminal_state(), marked state absorbing resulting transition matrix — every transition "Start" goes one original states (actor's first observed state), "Start" row row-stochastic exactly data dictates.

title: Mark leading-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_first_state
code: M <- mark_first_state(trajectories, state = "Start") # In a chain built from M, "Start" is a transient entry point.

what: Mark terminal-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_terminal_state
title: Mark terminal-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_terminal_state
text: Replaces every cell row's last observed state label given state, leaving non-terminal NAs untouched. result, passed build_network(), yields Markov chain marked state absorbing construction (P[state, state] = 1).

title: Mark terminal-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_terminal_state
code: mark_terminal_state(data, state = "End", cols = NULL)

title: Mark terminal-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_terminal_state
text: data wide-format matrix data.frame (rows = actors, cols = time steps) state labels NA missing observations. state Character. Label insert terminal-NA cells. Default "End". cols Optional state-column names; otherwise columns.

title: Mark terminal-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_terminal_state
text: data.frame shape data terminal NAs filled state.

title: Mark terminal-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_terminal_state
text: small piece pre-processing required turn right-censored sequence data absorbing-chain model. chain resulting matrix one extra state (state) structurally absorbing every cell actor's last observed step set state — chain stays forever entered. Use chain_structure() result compute mean absorption time, absorption probabilities, per-state classification. Note markov_stability() right summary absorbing chains; stationary distribution collapse absorbing state.

title: Mark terminal-NA cells with an explicit state label. — mark_terminal_state
code: M <- mark_terminal_state(trajectories, state = "Dropout") net <- build_network(M, method = "relative") chain_structure(net) #> Chain structure  [4 states, 2 communicating classes] #>   irreducible: FALSE   aperiodic: TRUE   regular: FALSE   reversible: NA #>   recurrent classes: 1   transient classes: 1 #>   absorbing states: Dropout #>  #> Use summary(x) for the per-state table, plot(x) for the heatmap.

what: Test the Markov order of a sequential process — markov_order_test
title: Test the Markov order of a sequential process — markov_order_test
text: Principled test whether categorical sequence best described \(k\)-th order Markov chain. order \(k = 1, \ldots,\) max_order, function computes classical likelihood-ratio statistic (\(G^2\)) conditional independence \(s \perp x \mid w\), \(w\) \((k-1)\)-gram context, \(x\) extra (k-th-back) state added order \(k\), \(s\) next state. \(H_0\) (order-\((k-1)\) correct), \(s\) independent \(x\) given \(w\). null distribution obtained exact within-\(w\) permutation test: context \(w\) successor labels exchangeable \(H_0\), shuffling \(s\) within \(w\)-group yields exact reference distribution \(G^2\). plug-MLE bias refitting per replicate. asymptotic \(\chi^2\) p-value reported alongside reference. optimal order smallest \(k\) significantly better \(k - 1\) level alpha: keep raising order test rejects, stop first non-rejection.

title: Test the Markov order of a sequential process — markov_order_test
code: markov_order_test(   data,   max_order = 3L,   n_perm = 500L,   alpha = 0.05,   parallel = FALSE,   n_cores = 2L,   seed = NULL )

title: Test the Markov order of a sequential process — markov_order_test
text: data data.frame (wide format, one sequence per row) list character vectors (one per trajectory). NAs treated end sequence. max_order Integer. Highest Markov order test. Default 3. n_perm Integer. Number within-\(w\) permutations per order. Default 500. alpha Numeric. Significance level order selection. Default 0.05. parallel Logical. Use parallel::mclapply permutations. Default FALSE (set TRUE Unix-like systems). n_cores Integer. Cores parallel execution. Default 2. seed Optional integer seed reproducibility.

title: Test the Markov order of a sequential process — markov_order_test
text: object class net_markov_order elements: optimal_order Integer. Selected order via sequential permutation test. bic_order Integer. Order minimising BIC (reported print/plot; used permutation selection). aic_order Integer. Order minimising AIC (reported print/plot; used permutation selection). test_table Tidy data.frame, one row per order tested columns order, loglik, AIC, BIC, df, g2, p_permutation, p_asymptotic, significant. AIC/BIC information-criterion values used ic plot panel derive aic_order/bic_order; order-0 row NA test columns (df, g2, p_permutation, p_asymptotic, significant). permutation_null List numeric vectors (length max_order), one empirical null \(G^2\) distribution per order. logliks Named numeric vector log-likelihoods per order (AIC / BIC panel , used test). layer_dofs Named integer vector model degrees freedom per order (free parameters added layer), used compute AIC / BIC columns. transition_matrices List fitted transition matrices. states Character vector observed state labels. n_sequences, n_observations Data summary. n_perm, alpha, max_order Call settings.

title: Test the Markov order of a sequential process — markov_order_test
code: # \donttest{ # First-order Markov data: test should select order 1 set.seed(1) states <- letters[1:4] tm <- matrix(runif(16), 4, 4, dimnames = list(states, states)) tm <- tm / rowSums(tm) seqs <- lapply(1:30, function(.) {   s <- character(50); s[1] <- sample(states, 1)   for (i in 2:50) s[i] <- sample(states, 1, prob = tm[s[i - 1], ])   s }) res <- markov_order_test(seqs, max_order = 3, n_perm = 300, seed = 1) res$optimal_order #> [1] 1 summary(res) #>   order    loglik      AIC      BIC  df        g2 p_permutation p_asymptotic #> 1     0 -2011.025 4028.050 4043.990  NA        NA            NA           NA #> 2     1 -1805.112 3640.224 3719.922   9 411.78019   0.003322259 4.193315e-83 #> 3     2 -1789.863 3703.726 4033.146  36  30.35171   0.803986711 7.337874e-01 #> 4     3 -1701.468 3808.936 4887.520 136 173.56575   0.099667774 1.633824e-02 #>   significant #> 1          NA #> 2        TRUE #> 3       FALSE #> 4       FALSE plot(res)  # }

title: Markov Stability Analysis — markov_stability
text: Computes per-state stability metrics transition network: persistence (self-loop probability), stationary distribution, mean recurrence time, sojourn time, mean accessibility /states.

title: Markov Stability Analysis — markov_stability
code: markov_stability(x, normalize = TRUE)  # S3 method for class 'net_markov_stability' plot(   x,   metrics = c("persistence", "stationary_prob", "return_time", "sojourn_time",     "avg_time_to_others", "avg_time_from_others"),   combined = TRUE,   ... )

title: Markov Stability Analysis — markov_stability
text: x netobject, cograph_network, tna object, row-stochastic numeric transition matrix, wide sequence data.frame (rows = actors, columns = time-steps). normalize Logical. Normalize rows sum 1? Default TRUE. metrics Character vector. metrics plot. Options: "persistence", "stationary_prob", "return_time", "sojourn_time", "avg_time_to_others", "avg_time_from_others". Default: six. combined TRUE (default), selected metrics shown one ggplot via facet_wrap(~ metric). FALSE, returns named list single-panel ggplots, one per metric, can printed, saved, re-laid-independently. ... Ignored.

title: Markov Stability Analysis — markov_stability
text: object class "net_markov_stability" : stability Data frame one row per state columns: state, persistence (\(P_{ii}\)), stationary_prob (\(\pi_i\)), return_time (\(1/\pi_i\)), sojourn_time (\(1/(1-P_{ii})\)), avg_time_to_others (mean MFPT leaving state \(\)), avg_time_from_others (mean MFPT arriving state \(\)). mpt underlying net_mpt object.

title: Markov Stability Analysis — markov_stability
text: Sojourn time expected consecutive time steps spent state leaving: \(1/(1-P_{ii})\). States persistence = 1 sojourn_time = Inf. avg_time_to_others: mean passage time state others; reflects "sticky" "isolated" state . avg_time_from_others: mean passage time states one; reflects accessibility (attractor strength).

title: Markov Stability Analysis — markov_stability
code: net <- build_network(as.data.frame(trajectories), method = "relative") ms  <- markov_stability(net) print(ms) #> Markov Stability Analysis #>  #>       state persistence stationary_prob return_time sojourn_time #>      Active      0.6976          0.3719        2.69         3.31 #>     Average      0.6099          0.4431        2.26         2.56 #>  Disengaged      0.4831          0.1850        5.41         1.93 #>  avg_time_to_others avg_time_from_others #>                6.99                 5.84 #>                6.74                 3.20 #>                4.47                 9.16 # \donttest{ plot(ms)  # }

what: Extract Transition Table from a MOGen Model — mogen_transitions
title: Extract Transition Table from a MOGen Model — mogen_transitions
text: Returns data frame transitions given Markov order, sorted count (descending). row shows full path readable sequence states, along observed count transition probability.

title: Extract Transition Table from a MOGen Model — mogen_transitions
code: mogen_transitions(x, order = NULL, min_count = 1L)

title: Extract Transition Table from a MOGen Model — mogen_transitions
text: x net_mogen object build_mogen(). order Integer. order's transitions extract. Must whole number; non-integer value error rather silently truncated. Defaults optimal order selected model. min_count Integer. Minimum observed count include (default 1). Use filter rare transitions unreliable probabilities.

title: Extract Transition Table from a MOGen Model — mogen_transitions
text: data frame columns: path full state sequence (e.g., "AI -> FAIL -> SOLVE"). count Number times transition observed. probability Transition probability P(| ). context / conditioning states (k-gram source node). predicted next state.

title: Extract Transition Table from a MOGen Model — mogen_transitions
text: order k, edge De Bruijn graph represents (k+1)-step path. example, order 2, edge node "AI -> FAIL" node "FAIL -> SOLVE" represents three-step path AI -> FAIL -> SOLVE. path column reconstructs full sequence readability.

title: Extract Transition Table from a MOGen Model — mogen_transitions
code: seqs <- list(c("A","B","C","D"), c("A","B","C","A"), c("B","C","D","A")) mg <- build_mogen(seqs, max_order = 2) mogen_transitions(mg, order = 1) #>     path count probability from to #> 1 B -> C     3      1.0000    B  C #> 2 A -> B     2      1.0000    A  B #> 3 C -> D     2      0.6667    C  D #> 4 C -> A     1      0.3333    C  A #> 5 D -> A     1      1.0000    D  A  # \donttest{ trajs <- list(c("A","B","C","D"), c("A","B","D","C"),               c("B","C","D","A"), c("C","D","A","B")) m <- build_mogen(trajs, max_order = 3) mogen_transitions(m, order = 1) #>     path count probability from to #> 1 A -> B     3      1.0000    A  B #> 2 C -> D     3      1.0000    C  D #> 3 D -> A     2      0.6667    D  A #> 4 B -> C     2      0.6667    B  C #> 5 D -> C     1      0.3333    D  C #> 6 B -> D     1      0.3333    B  D # }

what: Two-variable mosaic analysis (chi-square test + flat mosaic) — mosaic_analysis
title: Two-variable mosaic analysis (chi-square test + flat mosaic) — mosaic_analysis
text: Analyses association two categorical columns data.frame. Builds contingency table, drops sparse categories min_count, runs Pearson chi-square test (Fisher's exact test), computes Cramer's V df-adjusted effect-size label, draws flat ggplot2 mosaic whose tile area encodes counts whose fill encodes standardized Pearson residual (Nestimate diverging palette). tabular output tidy one-row-per-cell data.frame.

title: Two-variable mosaic analysis (chi-square test + flat mosaic) — mosaic_analysis
code: mosaic_analysis(   data,   var1,   var2,   min_count = 10L,   test = c("chisq", "fisher"),   percentage_base = c("total", "row", "column"),   tile_label = c("count", "percent", "residual", "category", "none"),   title = "",   ... )

title: Two-variable mosaic analysis (chi-square test + flat mosaic) — mosaic_analysis
text: data data.frame containing two variables. var1 Character. Name first variable (mosaic columns). var2 Character. Name second variable (stacked within columns). min_count Integer. Minimum marginal count category kept. Categories either variable dropped testing. Default 10. test Character. "chisq" (default) Pearson chi-square, "fisher" Fisher's exact test (simulated p-value). Cramer's V residual fill always derived chi-square statistic. percentage_base Character. Base "percent" tile label pct column: "total" (default), "row" (within var1), "column" (within var2). tile_label Character. print inside tile: "count" (default), "percent", "residual", "category" (var2 level), "none". title Character. Plot title. Default "". ... flat-mosaic styling arguments passed renderer (e.g. col_label_side, row_label_side, legend_position, legend_size, label_size, palette). Tile fill uses ColorBrewer RdBu ramp default (override palette). Column labels auto-rotate vertical 6 columns; pass col_label_angle force angle.

title: Two-variable mosaic analysis (chi-square test + flat mosaic) — mosaic_analysis
text: object class "mosaic_analysis": list plot flat mosaic ggplot object. counts Tidy data.frame, one row per (var1, var2) cell, observed, expected, residual (standardized), pct (percentage_base). stats One-row data.frame: test, statistic, df, p_value, cramers_v, effect_size, n. test raw htest object. cramers_v, effect_size Effect size value label. table filtered contingency table. removed List dropped var1 / var2 categories. n_original, n_filtered Row counts /filtering.

title: Two-variable mosaic analysis (chi-square test + flat mosaic) — mosaic_analysis
code: df <- data.frame(   gender = sample(c("F", "M"), 200, replace = TRUE),   level  = sample(c("Low", "Mid", "High"), 200, replace = TRUE) ) res <- mosaic_analysis(df, "gender", "level", min_count = 5) res$stats #>         test statistic df p_value cramers_v effect_size   n #> 1 Chi-square     0.848  2  0.6545     0.065  negligible 200 res$counts #>   gender level observed expected residual  pct #> 1      F  High       33   30.030    0.897 16.5 #> 2      M  High       33   35.970   -0.897 16.5 #> 3      F   Low       31   33.215   -0.653 15.5 #> 4      M   Low       42   39.785    0.653 21.0 #> 5      F   Mid       27   27.755   -0.233 13.5 #> 6      M   Mid       34   33.245    0.233 17.0 # \donttest{ plot(res, tile_label = "percent")  # }

what: Mosaic Plot of a Network's Transition or Co-occurrence Counts — mosaic_plot
title: Mosaic Plot of a Network's Transition or Co-occurrence Counts — mosaic_plot
text: Draws Hartigan-Friendly mosaic (marimekko geometry, chi-square standardized-residual fill) integer-weighted network. Equivalent algorithm appearance tna::plot_mosaic(); named differently avoid export clash packages attached.

title: Mosaic Plot of a Network's Transition or Co-occurrence Counts — mosaic_plot
code: mosaic_plot(x, ...)  # Default S3 method mosaic_plot(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'netobject' mosaic_plot(   x,   xlab = NULL,   ylab = NULL,   range = NULL,   top_angle = NULL,   left_angle = NULL,   residuals = c("permutation", "asymptotic"),   n_perm = 500L,   seed = NULL,   values = FALSE,   style = c("classic", "flat"),   ... )  # S3 method for class 'htna' mosaic_plot(   x,   xlab = NULL,   ylab = NULL,   range = NULL,   top_angle = NULL,   left_angle = NULL,   residuals = c("permutation", "asymptotic"),   n_perm = 500L,   seed = NULL,   values = FALSE,   style = c("classic", "flat"),   ... )  # S3 method for class 'mcml' mosaic_plot(   x,   level = c("macro", "clusters"),   xlab = NULL,   ylab = NULL,   range = NULL,   top_angle = NULL,   left_angle = NULL,   residuals = c("permutation", "asymptotic"),   n_perm = 500L,   seed = NULL,   ncol = 2L,   values = FALSE,   style = c("classic", "flat"),   ... )  # S3 method for class 'netobject_group' mosaic_plot(   x,   xlab = NULL,   ylab = NULL,   range = NULL,   top_angle = NULL,   left_angle = NULL,   residuals = c("permutation", "asymptotic"),   n_perm = 500L,   seed = NULL,   ncol = 2L,   values = FALSE,   style = c("classic", "flat"),   ... )  # S3 method for class 'table' mosaic_plot(   x,   xlab = "Row",   ylab = "Column",   range = NULL,   top_angle = NULL,   left_angle = NULL,   residuals = c("permutation", "asymptotic"),   n_perm = 500L,   seed = NULL,   values = FALSE,   style = c("classic", "flat"),   ... )  # S3 method for class 'matrix' mosaic_plot(x, ...)

title: Mosaic Plot of a Network's Transition or Co-occurrence Counts — mosaic_plot
text: x One four data-bearing Nestimate classes: netobject (single mosaic $weights), netobject_group (one panel per group), mcml (-cluster mosaic default; per-cluster panels level = "within"), htna (single mosaic $weights; htna inherits netobject geometry matches). Also accepts contingency table plain numeric matrix ad-hoc plotting. ... Flat styling overrides forwarded flat renderer style = "flat" (otherwise ignored). xlab, ylab Axis labels. NULL (default) draws axis title. Pass string add one. range Numeric length 2 giving lower upper colour-scale limits standardized residual. NULL (default) auto-fits limits symmetric range c(-M, M) M = max(|stdres|), signal squished. Pass explicit range (e.g. c(-4, 4) tna-style display, c(-6, 6) moderate clipping) clamp colour scale. top_angle, left_angle Rotation degrees top (x) left (y) tick labels. NULL (default) uses auto rule 90 n_levels > 3 else 0 axis. Pass numeric override (e.g. top_angle = 45, left_angle = 0). residuals One "permutation" (default) "asymptotic". "permutation" computes empirical-null z-scores shuffling one variable's labels n_perm draws reporting (O - mean_perm) / sd_perm per cell. Robust sparse tables. "asymptotic" returns stats::chisq.test()$stdres (closed-form (O - E) / sqrt(E*(1 - p_row)*(1 - p_col)) vcd tna use). n_perm Number permutations residuals = "permutation". Default 500; use >= 1000 stable tail estimates. seed Optional integer seed permutation RNG. Use reproducible plots; ignored residuals = "asymptotic". values Logical. TRUE, overlay cell's standardized residual numeric label (one decimal). Text colour switches white saturated cells (|stdres| > 1.5) dark grey otherwise. Default FALSE – colour bar legend already conveys sign magnitude. style Character. title: Network Comparison Test — nct
text: Tests whether two networks estimated independent samples differ three levels: global strength (M-statistic), network structure (S-statistic, max absolute edge difference), individual edges (E-statistic per edge). Inference via permutation group labels.

title: Network Comparison Test — nct
code: nct(   data1,   data2,   iter = 1000L,   gamma = 0.5,   paired = FALSE,   abs = TRUE,   weighted = TRUE,   p_adjust = "none" )

title: Network Comparison Test — nct
text: data1 numeric matrix data.frame observations group 1. data2 numeric matrix data.frame observations group 2. number columns data1. iter Integer. Number permutation iterations. Default 1000. gamma EBIC tuning parameter glasso. Default 0.5. paired Logical. TRUE, perform paired permutation (within-subject swap). Default FALSE. abs Logical. TRUE, compute global strength absolute edge weights. Default TRUE. weighted Logical. TRUE, use weighted networks tests. FALSE, binarize computing statistics. Default TRUE. p_adjust P-value adjustment method per-edge tests (method stats::p.adjust.methods). Default "none".

title: Network Comparison Test — nct
text: list class net_nct elements: nw1, nw2 Estimated weighted adjacency matrices. M List observed, perm, p_value global strength test. S structure maximum absolute edge difference. E structure per-edge tests. n_iter Number permutations. paired Whether paired test used.

title: Network Comparison Test — nct
text: Implementation matches NetworkComparisonTest::NCT() defaults abs = TRUE, weighted = TRUE, paired = FALSE machine precision seed used. network estimator EBIC-selected glasso applied Pearson correlation matrix, Matrix::nearPD symmetrization (matching NCT's NCT_estimator_GGM default).

title: Network Comparison Test — nct
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ set.seed(1) x1 <- matrix(rnorm(200 * 5), 200, 5) x2 <- matrix(rnorm(200 * 5), 200, 5) colnames(x1) <- colnames(x2) <- paste0("V", 1:5) res <- nct(x1, x2, iter = 100) res$M$p_value res$S$p_value } # }

title: Aggregate Edge Weights — net_aggregate_weights

title: Aggregate Edge Weights — net_aggregate_weights
text: w Numeric vector finite edge weights. NA zero weights excluded aggregation, every method (including "density", "min", "max", "prod", "geomean") operates non-zero, non-NA subset. method Single aggregation method: "sum", "mean", "median", "max", "min", "prod", "density", "geomean". zeros stripped first, "density" (sum(w) / n_possible) "mean" (sum(w) / number non-zero edges) return value whenever block fully dense – .e. count non-zero edges equals n_possible. diverge zero/NA edges present ("density" divides larger n_possible, "mean" smaller non-zero count). n_possible Optional single finite numeric number possible edges density calculation. omitted, "density" falls back sum(w) / length(w) non-zero subset (equivalent "mean"); supply n_possible (e.g. block size n_i * n_j) true edge density.

title: Mean First Passage Times — passage_time
text: Computes full matrix mean first passage times (MFPT) Markov chain. Element \(M_{ij}\) expected number steps travel state \(\) state \(j\) first time. diagonal equals mean recurrence time \(1/\pi_i\).

title: Mean First Passage Times — passage_time
code: passage_time(x, states = NULL, normalize = TRUE)  # S3 method for class 'net_mpt' summary(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'net_mpt' plot(   x,   log_scale = TRUE,   digits = 1,   title = "Mean First Passage Times",   low = "#004d00",   high = "#ccffcc",   ... )

title: Mean First Passage Times — passage_time
text: x netobject, cograph_network, tna object, row-stochastic numeric transition matrix, wide sequence data.frame (rows = actors, columns = time-steps; relative transition network built automatically). states Character vector. Restrict output states. NULL (default) keeps states. normalize Logical. TRUE (default), rows sum 1 normalized automatically (warning). object net_mpt object (summary). ... Ignored. log_scale Logical. Apply log transform fill scale better contrast? Default TRUE. digits Integer. Decimal places displayed cells. Default 1. title Character. Plot title. low Character. Hex colour low end (short passage time). Default dark green "#004d00". high Character. Hex colour high end (long passage time). Default pale green "#ccffcc".

title: Mean First Passage Times — passage_time
text: object class "net_mpt" : matrix Full \(n \times n\) MFPT matrix. Row \(\), column \(j\) = expected steps state \(\) state \(j\). Diagonal = mean recurrence time \(1/\pi_i\). stationary Named numeric vector: stationary distribution \(\pi\). return_times Named numeric vector: \(1/\pi_i\) per state. states Character vector state names. summary.net_mpt returns data frame one row per state columns state, return_time, stationary, mean_out (mean steps states), mean_in (mean steps states).

title: Mean First Passage Times — passage_time
text: Uses Kemeny-Snell fundamental matrix formula: $$M_{ij} = \frac{Z_{jj} - Z_{ij}}{\pi_j}, \quad       Z = (- P + \Pi)^{-1}$$ \(\Pi_{ij} = \pi_j\). Requires ergodic (irreducible, aperiodic) chain.

title: Mean First Passage Times — passage_time
text: Kemeny, J.G. Snell, J.L. (1976). Finite Markov Chains. Springer-Verlag.

title: Mean First Passage Times — passage_time
code: net <- build_network(as.data.frame(trajectories), method = "relative") pt  <- passage_time(net) print(pt) #> Mean First Passage Times (3 states) #>  #>            Active Average Disengaged #> Active        2.7     3.6       10.4 #> Average       5.5     2.3        8.0 #> Disengaged    6.2     2.8        5.4 #>  #> Stationary distribution: #>     Active    Average Disengaged  #>     0.3719     0.4431     0.1850  # \donttest{ plot(pt)  # }

what: Per-Context Path Dependence at Order k — path_dependence
title: Per-Context Path Dependence at Order k — path_dependence
text: Diagnoses chain's order-1 Markov assumption fails comparing, order-k context \((s_1, \ldots, s_{k-1})\), empirical next-state distribution \(P(s_k \mid s_1, \ldots, s_{k-1})\) order-1 prediction \(P(s_k \mid s_{k-1})\) uses recent state. Returns tidy per-context table sorted Kullback-Leibler divergence analyst can see exactly histories carry extra predictive information.

title: Per-Context Path Dependence at Order k — path_dependence
code: path_dependence(x, order = 2L, min_count = 5L, base = 2)

title: Per-Context Path Dependence at Order k — path_dependence
text: x wide sequence data.frame / matrix (rows = actors, columns = time-steps), netobject carries source data. order Integer. Order conditioning context. order = 2 (default) compares 2-step memory 1-step; order = 3 compares 3-step memory; etc. Must whole number; non-integer value error rather silently truncated. min_count Integer. Drop contexts seen fewer many times. Default 5. small samples produce noisy KL estimates. base Numeric. Logarithm base entropy KL. Default 2 (bits).

title: Per-Context Path Dependence at Order k — path_dependence
text: object class "net_path_dependence" contexts tidy data.frame, one row per order-k context, sorted KL descending. Columns: context (e.g. "-> B"), n (count), H_order1 (entropy \(P(\cdot \mid s_{k-1})\)), H_orderk (entropy \(P(\cdot \mid \mathrm{context})\)), H_drop (= H_order1 - H_orderk), KL (= \(D_{KL}(P_k \| P_1)\)), top_o1 (likely next state order-1), top_ok (likely next state order-k), flips (logical: likely next state change?). chain list chain-level summaries: KL_weighted (count-weighted mean KL across contexts), H_drop_weighted (count-weighted mean entropy drop), n_contexts, n_flips (contexts -likely next state changed). order integer base numeric min_count integer states character vector

title: Per-Context Path Dependence at Order k — path_dependence
text: context \(c = (s_1, \ldots, s_{k-1})\) occurring least min_count times, function computes: empirical conditional \(P_k(\cdot \mid c)\) k-gram counts; order-1 prediction \(P_1(\cdot \mid s_{k-1})\) recent state alone (bigram-marginal estimator); entropy drop \(H(P_1) - H(P_k)\) - bits uncertainty removed extending memory one step specific context; Kullback-Leibler divergence \(D_{KL}(P_k \,\|\, P_1)\) - bits "surprise" used order-1 model order-k model true. KL = 0 means longer history adds information context. H_drop > 0 means longer history sharpens prediction; H_drop < 0 indicates order-k context happens spread probability across outcomes order-1 alone (small-sample noise genuine context-induced uncertainty - inspect n). Contexts flips = TRUE substantively interesting ones: longer history changes modal prediction, just confidence. Pair markov_order_test (decides whether order-k needed globally) see chain-level decision broken per context.

title: Per-Context Path Dependence at Order k — path_dependence

what: Permutation Test for Network Comparison — permutation
title: Permutation Test for Network Comparison — permutation
text: Compares two networks estimated build_network using permutation test. Works built-methods (transition association) well custom registered estimators. test shuffles observations belong group, re-estimates networks, tests whether observed edge-wise differences exceed chance. transition methods ("relative", "frequency", "co_occurrence"), uses fast pre-computation strategy: per-sequence count matrices computed , permutation iteration shuffles group labels computes group-wise colSums. association methods ("cor", "pcor", "glasso", custom estimators), full estimator called permuted group split. either transition network contains one sequence, function warns network recommended permutation confirmatory testing.

Significance level (default: 0.05). paired Logical. TRUE, permute within pairs (requires equal number observations x y). Default: FALSE. adjust Character. p-value adjustment method passed p.adjust (default: "none"). Common choices: "holm", "BH", "bonferroni". nlambda Integer. Number lambda values glassopath regularisation path (used method = "glasso"). Higher values give finer lambda resolution cost speed. Default: 50. seed Integer NULL. RNG seed reproducibility.

title: Permutation Test for Network Comparison — permutation
Persistence Landscape — persistence_landscape
text: Computes persistence landscape (Bubenik 2015) persistence diagram. (birth, death) pair contributes tent function $$\Lambda_{(b,d)}(t) = \max(0, \min(t - b, d - t)).$$ \(k\)-th landscape function \(\lambda^{(k)}(t)\) \(k\)-th largest \(\{\Lambda_{(b_i,d_i)}(t)\}_i\) \(t\). Landscapes stable bottleneck distance form Banach-space embedding persistence diagrams.

title: Persistence Landscape — persistence_landscape
code: persistence_landscape(ph, k_max = 5L, dimension = 1L, t_grid = NULL)

title: Persistence Landscape — persistence_landscape
text: ph persistent_homology object data.frame columns dimension, birth, death. k_max Maximum landscape index compute (default 5). Must single positive integer. dimension Integer scalar – homology dimension compute landscape . Default 1. t_grid Numeric vector evaluation points. NULL (default) uses even grid 200 points covering union pair intervals.

title: Persistence Landscape — persistence_landscape
text: persistence_landscape object : landscape Data frame: k, t, value. dimension Integer scalar. k_max Integer scalar. t_grid Numeric vector.

title: Persistence Landscape — persistence_landscape
text: Bubenik, P. (2015). Statistical topological data analysis using persistence landscapes. Journal Machine Learning Research 16, 77-102.

title: Persistence Landscape — persistence_landscape
code: mat <- matrix(c(0, .6, .5, .6, 0, .4, .5, .4, 0), 3, 3) rownames(mat) <- colnames(mat) <- c("A","B","C") ph <- persistent_homology(mat, n_steps = 5) pl <- persistence_landscape(ph, k_max = 3, dimension = 0)

title: Persistent Homology — persistent_homology
text: Computes persistent homology via full boundary-matrix reduction \(\mathbb{Z}/2\) (Edelsbrunner, Letscher & Zomorodian 2000). returned persistence diagram pairs k-dimensional homology class simplex whose addition creates (birth) simplex whose addition destroys (death). Essential classes — never killed — reported death = 0 clique mode (similarity scale, descending) death = Inf VR mode (distance scale, ascending). Two filtration modes supported: type = "clique" Weighted clique filtration. Input treated similarity matrix; high-weight simplices appear early. k-simplex \(\sigma\), filtration value \(\min_{(,j) \\sigma}\,|w(,j)|\). Thresholds run high low. type = "vr" Vietoris-Rips filtration non-negative distance matrix. k-simplex \(\sigma\), filtration value \(\max_{(,j) \\sigma}\,d(,j)\). Thresholds run low high. Use max_scale cap filtration diameter.

title: Persistent Homology — persistent_homology
code: persistent_homology(   x,   n_steps = 20L,   max_dim = 3L,   type = "clique",   max_scale = NULL )

title: Persistent Homology — persistent_homology
text: x square matrix, tna, netobject. type = "vr", must non-negative distance matrix. n_steps Number grid points reported Betti curve (default 20). persistence diagram exact — depend n_steps. max_dim Maximum simplex dimension track (default 3). type Filtration: "clique" (default, similarity-weighted) "vr" (Vietoris-Rips distances). max_scale type = "vr" : cap edge length. Edges d(,j) > max_scale excluded. NULL (default) uses max(d).

title: Persistent Homology — persistent_homology
text: persistent_homology object : betti_curve Data frame: threshold, dimension, betti. persistence Data frame birth-death pairs: dimension, birth, death, persistence. Sorted descending persistence. thresholds Numeric vector grid thresholds. mode Either "clique" "vr".

title: Persistent Homology — persistent_homology
text: Edelsbrunner, H., Letscher, D., & Zomorodian, . (2000). Topological persistence simplification. Discrete & Computational Geometry 28, 511-533.

title: Persistent Homology — persistent_homology
code: mat <- matrix(c(0,.6,.5,.6,0,.4,.5,.4,0), 3, 3) colnames(mat) <- rownames(mat) <- c("A","B","C") ph <- persistent_homology(mat, n_steps = 10) print(ph) #> Persistent Homology #>   10 filtration steps [0.6000 → 0.0060] #>   Features: b0: 2 (1 persistent)  #>   Longest-lived: #>     b0: 0.6000 → 0.0000 (life: 0.6000) #>     b0: 0.6000 → 0.5000 (life: 0.1000)

title: Plot Method for cluster_choice — plot.cluster_choice
text: Type cheat-sheet: "auto" Default. Picks one others based axes swept. k--> "lines"; one categorical axis swept -> "bars"; k plus one categorical -> "lines"; k plus two categoricals -> "facet"; categoricals without k -> "heatmap". "lines" Silhouette across k (mean_within_dist k swept axis), one line per non-k axis present. "bars" Horizontal bar chart silhouette per axis level. Bars sorted silhouette. "heatmap" Tiled silhouette across two categorical axes. Requires dissimilarity method swept. "tradeoff" Scatter: silhouette (y) vs size_ratio (x). Works sweep; labels point. "facet" Lines vs k, colour one categorical axis, facet another. Requires k plus two categoricals. Asking type data support raises error pointing alternatives.

code: # \donttest{ # First-order Markov data: test should select order 1 set.seed(1) states <- letters[1:4] tm <- matrix(runif(16), 4, 4, dimnames = list(states, states)) tm <- tm / rowSums(tm) seqs <- lapply(1:30, function(.) {   s <- character(50); s[1] <- sample(states, 1)   for (i in 2:50) s[i] <- sample(states, 1, prob = tm[s[i - 1], ])   s }) res <- markov_order_test(seqs, max_order = 3, n_perm = 300, seed = 1) res$optimal_order #> [1] 1 summary(res) #>   order    loglik      AIC      BIC  df        g2 p_permutation p_asymptotic #> 1     0 -2011.025 4028.050 4043.990  NA        NA            NA           NA #> 2     1 -1805.112 3640.224 3719.922   9 411.78019   0.003322259 4.193315e-83 #> 3     2 -1789.863 3703.726 4033.146  36  30.35171   0.803986711 7.337874e-01 #> 4     3 -1701.468 3808.936 4887.520 136 173.56575   0.099667774 1.633824e-02 #>   significant #> 1          NA #> 2        TRUE #> 3       FALSE #> 4       FALSE plot(res)  # }

code: plot_state_frequencies(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'netobject' plot_state_frequencies(   x,   style = "marimekko",   metric = "prop",   label = "prop",   legend = "auto",   legend_dir = "auto",   legend_frame = "none",   sort_states = "frequency",   colors = NULL,   label_size = 3.5,   abbreviate = FALSE,   include_macro = FALSE,   combine = "auto",   ncol = NULL,   node_groups = NULL,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'htna' plot_state_frequencies(   x,   style = "marimekko",   metric = "prop",   label = "prop",   legend = "auto",   legend_dir = "auto",   legend_frame = "none",   sort_states = "frequency",   colors = NULL,   label_size = 3.5,   abbreviate = FALSE,   include_macro = FALSE,   combine = "auto",   ncol = NULL,   node_groups = NULL,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'mcml' plot_state_frequencies(   x,   style = "marimekko",   metric = "prop",   label = "prop",   legend = "auto",   legend_dir = "auto",   legend_frame = "none",   sort_states = "frequency",   colors = NULL,   label_size = 3.5,   abbreviate = FALSE,   include_macro = FALSE,   combine = "auto",   ncol = NULL,   node_groups = NULL,   ... )  # S3 method for class 'netobject_group' plot_state_frequencies(   x,   style = "marimekko",   metric = "prop",   label = "prop",   legend = "auto",   legend_dir = "auto",   legend_frame = "none",   sort_states = "frequency",   colors = NULL,   label_size = 3.5,   abbreviate = FALSE,   include_macro = FALSE,   combine = "auto",   ncol = NULL,   node_groups = NULL,   ... )  # Default S3 method plot_state_frequencies(x, ...)

title: Plot State Frequency Distributions — plot_state_frequencies
text: x netobject, netobject_group, mcml, htna object. ... Reserved future use. style One : "marimekko" (default) – per-group treemap panels cumulative-width geometry; tile area = within-group state share. "bars" – horizontal bars sorted frequency, faceted per group. chi-square mosaics (group x state) contingency table, use mosaic_plot directly – kept separate function dispatch surface. metric style = "bars": value bar length encodes – "prop" (default) "freq". Treemap hierarchical-marimekko areas always encode proportion within group. label Inline tile / bar annotation. formats render single line. "prop" (default) – proportion , e.g. "66%" "freq" – count , e.g. "1,234" "" – count + proportion, e.g. "1,234 (66%)" "state" – state name , e.g. "Average" "" – state + proportion, e.g. "Average (66%)" "none" – inline labels legend Legend position. "auto" (default) resolves per style: "none" style = "bars" (y-axis already names every state, colour legend redundant); "per_facet" htna/mcml treemaps (state vocabularies differ per panel, gets legend); "bottom" single-network netobject_group treemaps (shared state vocabulary, one shared legend). Override "bottom", "top", "right", "left", "none", "per_facet". "per_facet" option requires gridExtra package returns gtable. legend_dir Legend internal layout: "auto" (default – horizontal top/bottom, vertical left/right), force "horizontal" "vertical" regardless position. legend_frame "none" (default) unframed legend, "border" draw thin grey rectangle around legend ("legend enclosed square"). sort_states One "frequency" (default – frequent first), "alpha", "none". colors Optional character vector overriding default Okabe-Ito state palette. Length must least number unique states. label_size Numeric size inline labels (max size ggfittext installed – text auto-shrinks per tile). abbreviate Abbreviate state names. FALSE (default) shows full names; TRUE truncates first 3 characters via base::abbreviate() (extends truncation needed keep names unique collision); positive integer sets target minimum length explicitly (e.g. abbreviate = 4). Affects tile labels, legend, returned $table. include_macro mcml : prepend "macro" reference column showing aggregate state frequencies across clusters. Default FALSE. combine legend = "per_facet" . "auto" (default) returns single combined gtable 1-3 panels list ggplots (one per panel) 4+ panels – many-cluster mcml layouts read better separate figures tile grid. TRUE forces combined gtable via gridExtra; FALSE forces list (knitr renders chunk's full fig.width / fig.height). ncol legend = "per_facet" combine = TRUE: number columns grid arrangement. NULL (default) picks 1, 2, 3 columns based number panels. node_groups Optional named character vector mapping node labels semantic groups. supplied, panels (bars) coloured / annotated group rather individual state, state-level palettes can collapse onto smaller categorical legend.

title: Plot State Frequency Distributions — plot_state_frequencies
text: state_freq object: list rendered $plot (ggplot gtable), tidy $table (data.frame columns group, state, count, proportion), call's $style, $metric, $source_class. class supports print() (shows tidy table console), plot() (renders chart), .data.frame() (returns table).

title: Plot State Frequency Distributions — plot_state_frequencies
text: marimekko layout dispatched per class: mcml, states partition cleanly clusters, chart hierarchical 2D marimekko: cluster columns width proportional cluster total, segments stacked vertically heights proportional within-cluster state proportions. classes (netobject, netobject_group, htna), group rendered panel containing squarified treemap: state becomes rectangular tile whose AREA exactly proportional state's share within group. Single-panel groups exist; faceted groups present. bar style produces horizontal bars (state y-axis), faceted group groups exist. variants use Okabe-Ito palette.

title: Plot State Frequency Distributions — plot_state_frequencies
code: # \donttest{ if (requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   data(group_regulation_long, package = "Nestimate")   nw <- build_network(group_regulation_long,                       method = "relative", format = "long",                       actor = "Actor", action = "Action",                       order = "Time", group = "Course")   res <- plot_state_frequencies(nw)   print(res)            # tidy frequency table in the console   plot(res)             # ggplot chart   head(as.data.frame(res)) } #> State frequencies (style = marimekko, source = netobject_group) #>   Total events: 27,533  |  Groups: 3  |  States: 9 #>  #> Per-group totals #>     group  events  share #>     A      12,390  45.0% #>     B       9,626  35.0% #>     C       5,517  20.0%  #>  #> Per-state proportions (within group) #>     group  state       count  share #>     A      consensus   3,298  26.6% #>     A      plan        2,805  22.6% #>     A      discuss     1,960  15.8% #>     A      emotion     1,517  12.2% #>     A      cohesion      923  7.4% #>     A      coregulate    855  6.9% #>     A      monitor       602  4.9% #>     A      synthesis     290  2.3% #>     A      adapt         140  1.1% #>     B      plan        2,445  25.4% #>     B      consensus   2,226  23.1% #>     B      discuss     1,453  15.1% #>     B      emotion       995  10.3% #>     B      coregulate    817  8.5% #>     B      cohesion      590  6.1% #>     B      monitor       565  5.9% #>     B      synthesis     274  2.8% #>     B      adapt         261  2.7% #>     C      plan        1,373  24.9% #>     C      consensus   1,273  23.1% #>     C      discuss       854  15.5% #>     C      emotion       563  10.2% #>     C      coregulate    461  8.4% #>     C      monitor       349  6.3% #>     C      cohesion      326  5.9% #>     C      synthesis     165  3.0% #>     C      adapt         153  2.8%    #>   group      state count proportion #> 1     A  consensus  3298 0.26618241 #> 2     A       plan  2805 0.22639225 #> 3     A    discuss  1960 0.15819209 #> 4     A    emotion  1517 0.12243745 #> 5     A   cohesion   923 0.07449556 #> 6     A coregulate   855 0.06900726 # what: Predict Missing or Future Links in a Network — predict_links
title: Predict Missing or Future Links in a Network — predict_links
text: Computes link prediction scores node pairs using one structural similarity methods. Accepts netobject, mcml, cograph_network, raw weight matrix. methods fully vectorized using matrix operations — loops. Supports weighted binary adjacency, directed undirected networks.

title: Predict Missing or Future Links in a Network — predict_links
code: predict_links(   x,   methods = c("common_neighbors", "resource_allocation", "adamic_adar", "jaccard",     "preferential_attachment", "katz"),   weighted = TRUE,   top_n = NULL,   exclude_existing = TRUE,   include_self = FALSE,   katz_damping = NULL )

title: Predict Missing or Future Links in a Network — predict_links
text: x netobject, mcml, cograph_network, numeric square matrix. methods Character vector. One : "common_neighbors", "resource_allocation", "adamic_adar", "jaccard", "preferential_attachment", "katz". Default: six methods. weighted Logical. TRUE, use weight matrix directly instead binarizing. Default: TRUE. top_n Integer NULL. Return top N predictions per method. Default: NULL (pairs). exclude_existing Logical. TRUE, exclude node pairs already edge. Default: TRUE. include_self Logical. TRUE, include self-loop predictions. Default: FALSE. katz_damping Numeric NULL. Attenuation factor Katz index. NULL, auto-computed 0.9 / spectral_radius(). Default: NULL.

title: Predict Missing or Future Links in a Network — predict_links
text: object class "net_link_prediction" containing: predictions Data frame columns: , , method, score, rank. Sorted score (descending) within method. scores Named list score matrices (one per method). methods Character vector methods used. nodes Character vector node names. directed Logical. weighted Logical. n_nodes Integer. n_existing Integer. Number existing edges.

title: Predict Missing or Future Links in a Network — predict_links
text: common_neighbors Number shared neighbors. directed graphs, sums shared -neighbors shared -neighbors. Vectorized %*% t() + t() %*% . resource_allocation Zhou et al. (2009). Like common neighbors weights shared neighbor z 1/degree(z). Penalizes hubs, rewards rare shared connections. adamic_adar Adamic & Adar (2003). Like resource allocation weights 1/log(degree(z)). Less aggressive penalty RA. jaccard Ratio shared neighbors total neighbors. directed graphs, computed combined (+) neighbor sets. preferential_attachment Product source -degree target -degree. Captures "rich-get-richer" effect. katz Katz (1953). Weighted sum paths nodes, exponentially damped path length. Computed via matrix inversion: (- beta * )^{-1} - . Captures global structure.

title: Predict Missing or Future Links in a Network — predict_links
text: Liben-Nowell, D. & Kleinberg, J. (2007). link-prediction problem social networks. JASIST, 58(7), 1019–1031. Zhou, T., Lu, L. & Zhang, Y.-C. (2009). Network topology link prediction. European Physical Journal B, 71, 623–630. Adamic, L. . & Adar, E. (2003). Friends neighbors Web. Social Networks, 25(3), 211–230. Katz, L. (1953). new status index derived sociometric analysis. Psychometrika, 18(1), 39–43.

title: Predict Missing or Future Links in a Network — predict_links
code: seqs <- data.frame(   V1 = sample(LETTERS[1:5], 50, TRUE),   V2 = sample(LETTERS[1:5], 50, TRUE),   V3 = sample(LETTERS[1:5], 50, TRUE) ) net <- build_network(seqs, method = "relative") pred <- predict_links(net) print(pred) #> Link Prediction  [directed | weighted | 5 nodes | 19 existing edges] #>   Methods: common_neighbors, resource_allocation, adamic_adar, jaccard, preferential_attachment, katz #>  #>   Top predicted links (consensus across 6 methods): #>     1. E -> C  (avg rank: 1.0, agreed: 6/6) summary(pred) #>                    method n_predictions  score_mean score_sd   score_max #> 1        common_neighbors             1  0.39998149       NA  0.39998149 #> 2     resource_allocation             1  0.04079016       NA  0.04079016 #> 3             adamic_adar             1  0.17519419       NA  0.17519419 #> 4                 jaccard             1  0.09383030       NA  0.09383030 #> 5 preferential_attachment             1 16.00000000       NA 16.00000000 #> 6                    katz             1  1.10171908       NA  1.10171908 #>     score_min #> 1  0.39998149 #> 2  0.04079016 #> 3  0.17519419 #> 4  0.09383030 #> 5 16.00000000 #> 6  1.10171908

title: Compute Node Predictability — predictability
text: Computes proportion variance explained (R\(^2\)) node network, following Haslbeck & Waldorp (2018). method = "glasso" "pcor", predictability computed analytically precision matrix: $$R^2_j = 1 - 1 / \Omega_{jj}$$ \(\Omega\) precision (inverse correlation) matrix. method = "cor", predictability multiple R\(^2\) regressing node network neighbors (nodes non-zero edges).

title: Compute Node Predictability — predictability
code: predictability(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'netobject' predictability(object, data = NULL, ...)  # S3 method for class 'netobject_ml' predictability(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'netobject_group' predictability(object, ...)

title: Compute Node Predictability — predictability
text: object netobject netobject_ml object. ... Additional arguments (ignored). data Optional data frame original variables used estimate network. Required method = "cor" (multiple-R\(^2\) regression node neighbours); ignored precision-matrix path used glasso/pcor, need raw data.

title: Compute Node Predictability — predictability
text: netobject: named numeric vector R\(^2\) values (one per node, 0 1). netobject_ml: list elements $$within, named numeric vector. named numeric vector predictability values per node. list within predictability vectors. named list per-group predictability vectors.

title: Compute Node Predictability — predictability
text: Haslbeck, J. M. B., & Waldorp, L. J. (2018). well network models predict observations? importance predictability network models. Behavior Research Methods, 50(2), 853–861. doi:10.3758/s13428-017-0910-x

title: Compute Node Predictability — predictability
code: set.seed(42) mat <- matrix(rnorm(60), ncol = 4) colnames(mat) <- LETTERS[1:4] net <- build_network(as.data.frame(mat), method = "glasso") predictability(net) #>   node R2      RMSE #> 1    A  0 0.9904791 #> 2    B  0 1.3109578 #> 3    C  0 0.9630332 #> 4    D  0 1.0523124

what: Prepare Event Log Data for Network Estimation — prepare
title: Prepare Event Log Data for Network Estimation — prepare
text: Converts event log data (actor, action, time) wide sequence format suitable build_network. Automatically parses timestamps, detects sessions time gaps, handles tie-breaking.

title: Prepare Event Log Data for Network Estimation — prepare
code: prepare(   data,   actor,   action,   time = NULL,   order = NULL,   session = NULL,   time_threshold = 900,   custom_format = NULL,   is_unix_time = FALSE,   unix_time_unit = c("seconds", "milliseconds", "microseconds") )

title: Prepare Event Log Data for Network Estimation — prepare
text: data Data frame event log columns. actor Character character vector. Column name(s) identifying performed action (e.g. "student" c("student", "group")). missing, data treated one actor. action Character. Column name containing action/state/code. time Character NULL. Column name containing timestamps. Supports ISO8601, Unix timestamps (numeric), 40+ date/time formats. NULL, row order defines sequence. Default: NULL. order Character NULL. Column name tie-breaking timestamps identical. NULL, original row order used. Default: NULL. session Character, character vector, NULL. Column name(s) explicit session grouping (e.g. "course" c("course", "semester")). combined time, sessions split time gaps. Default: NULL. time_threshold Numeric. Maximum gap seconds consecutive events new session starts. used time provided. Default: 900 (15 minutes). custom_format Character NULL. Custom strptime format string parsing timestamps. Default: NULL (auto-detect). is_unix_time Logical. TRUE, treat numeric time values Unix timestamps. Default: FALSE (auto-detected numeric columns). unix_time_unit Character. Unit Unix timestamps: "seconds", "milliseconds", "microseconds". Default: "seconds".

title: Prepare Event Log Data for Network Estimation — prepare
text: list class "nestimate_data" containing: sequence_data Data frame wide format (one row per session, columns T1, T2, ...). long_data processed long-format data session IDs. meta_data Session-level metadata (session ID, actor). time_data Parsed time values wide format (time provided). statistics List total_sessions, total_actions, max_sequence_length, unique_actors, etc.

title: Prepare Event Log Data for Network Estimation — prepare
code: df <- data.frame(   student = rep(1:3, each = 5),   code = sample(c("read", "write", "test"), 15, replace = TRUE),   timestamp = seq.POSIXt(as.POSIXct("2024-01-01"), by = "min", length.out = 15) ) prepared <- prepare(df, actor = "student", action = "code",                     time = "timestamp") net <- build_network(prepared$sequence_data, method = "relative")

title: Prepare Data for TNA Analysis — prepare_for_tna
code: prepare_for_tna(   data,   type = c("sequences", "long", "auto"),   state_names = NULL,   id_col = "Actor",   time_col = "Time",   action_col = "Action",   validate = TRUE )

title: Prepare Data for TNA Analysis — prepare_for_tna
text: data Data frame containing sequence data. type Character. Type input data: "sequences" Wide format one row per sequence (default). "long" Long format one row per action. "auto" Automatically detect format based column names. state_names Character vector. Expected state names, NULL extract data. Default: NULL. id_col Character. Name ID column long format data. Default: "Actor". time_col Character. Name time column long format data. Default: "Time". action_col Character. Name action column long format data. Default: "Action". validate Logical. Whether validate actions state_names. Default: TRUE.

title: Prepare Data for TNA Analysis — prepare_for_tna
text: data frame ready use TNA functions. "sequences" type, returns data frame row sequence columns time points (V1, V2, ...). "long" type, converts wide format first.

title: Prepare Data for TNA Analysis — prepare_for_tna
text: function performs several preparations: Converts long format wide format needed. Validates actions/states recognized. Removes non-sequence columns (e.g., id, metadata). Converts factors characters. Ensures consistent column naming (V1, V2, ...).

title: Prepare Data for TNA Analysis — prepare_for_tna
code: # From wide format sequences sequences <- data.frame(   V1 = c("A","B","C","A"), V2 = c("B","C","A","B"),   V3 = c("C","A","B","C"), V4 = c("A","B","A","B") ) tna_data <- prepare_for_tna(sequences, type = "sequences")

what: Import One-Hot Encoded Data into Sequence Format — prepare_onehot
title: Import One-Hot Encoded Data into Sequence Format — prepare_onehot
text: Converts binary indicator (one-hot) data wide sequence format expected build_network tna::tna(). binary column represents state; rows value 1 marked column name. Supports optional windowed aggregation. Simultaneous active states preserved using window-span representation tna::import_onehot(): input row/window expanded one sequence slot per code transition counting occurs windows, simultaneous states inside row.

title: Import One-Hot Encoded Data into Sequence Format — prepare_onehot
code: prepare_onehot(   data,   cols,   actor = NULL,   session = NULL,   interval = NULL,   window_size = 3L,   window_type = c("non-overlapping", "overlapping"),   aggregate = FALSE )

title: Import One-Hot Encoded Data into Sequence Format — prepare_onehot
text: data Data frame binary (0/1) indicator columns. cols Character vector. Names one-hot columns use. actor Character NULL. Name actor/ID column. NULL, rows treated single sequence. Default: NULL. session Character NULL. Name session column sub-grouping within actors. Default: NULL. interval Integer NULL. Number rows per time point output. NULL, rows become single time point group. Default: NULL. window_size Integer (>= 1). Number consecutive rows aggregate window. Default: 3. Set window_size = 1 windowing (row time point). window_type Character. "non-overlapping" (fixed, separate windows) "overlapping" (rolling, step = 1). Default: "non-overlapping". aggregate Logical. TRUE, aggregate within window taking first non-NA indicator per column. Default: FALSE.

title: Import One-Hot Encoded Data into Sequence Format — prepare_onehot
text: data frame wide format columns named W{window}_T{time} cell contains state name NA. Attributes windowed, window_size, window_span set result.

title: Import One-Hot Encoded Data into Sequence Format — prepare_onehot
code: # Simple binary data df <- data.frame(   A = c(1, 0, 1, 0, 1),   B = c(0, 1, 0, 1, 0),   C = c(0, 0, 0, 0, 0) ) seq_data <- prepare_onehot(df, cols = c("A", "B", "C"))  # With actor grouping df$actor <- c(1, 1, 1, 2, 2) seq_data <- prepare_onehot(df, cols = c("A", "B", "C"), actor = "actor")  # With windowing seq_data <- prepare_onehot(df, cols = c("A", "B", "C"),                           window_size = 2, window_type = "non-overlapping")

code: set.seed(1) dat <- as.data.frame(matrix(rnorm(60), ncol = 3)) bg <- boot_glasso(dat, iter = 10, cs_iter = 5, centrality = "strength") print(bg) #> GLASSO Bootstrap (10 iterations, 5 case-drop per proportion) #>   Data: 20 x 3  |  Alpha: 0.05  |  Gamma: 0.50 #>   Edges: 0/3 significant (CI excludes zero) #>   Mean inclusion probability: 0.63 #>  #>   Centrality Stability (CS-coefficient): #>     strength:              0.00 [Unstable] #>  #>   Edge differences: 2/3 pairs significantly different #>   Timing: 0.9s (bootstrap: 0.5s, case-drop: 0.3s) # \donttest{ set.seed(42) mat <- matrix(rnorm(60), ncol = 4) colnames(mat) <- LETTERS[1:4] boot <- boot_glasso(as.data.frame(mat), iter = 20, cs_iter = 10,   centrality = "strength", seed = 42) print(boot) #> GLASSO Bootstrap (20 iterations, 10 case-drop per proportion) #>   Data: 15 x 4  |  Alpha: 0.05  |  Gamma: 0.50 #>   Edges: 0/6 significant (CI excludes zero) #>   Mean inclusion probability: 0.34 #>  #>   Centrality Stability (CS-coefficient): #>     strength:              0.00 [Unstable] #>  #>   Edge differences: 1/15 pairs significantly different #>   Timing: 3.4s (bootstrap: 2.1s, case-drop: 1.2s) # }

code: seqs <- data.frame(V1 = c("A","B","C","A"), V2 = c("B","C","A","B")) clusters <- list(G1 = c("A","B"), G2 = c("C")) cs <- build_mcml(seqs, clusters) print(cs) #> MCML Network #> ============ #> Type: tna  | Method: sum  #> Nodes: 3  | Clusters: 2  #> Transitions: 4  #>   Macro: 2  | Per-cluster: 2  #>  #> Clusters: #>   G1 (2): A, B #>   G2 (1): C #>  #> Macro (cluster-level) weights: #>        G1     G2 #> G1 0.6667 0.3333 #> G2 1.0000 0.0000 # \donttest{ seqs <- data.frame(   T1 = c("A","B","A"), T2 = c("B","C","B"),   T3 = c("C","A","C"), T4 = c("A","B","A") ) clusters <- c("Alpha", "Beta", "Alpha") cs <- build_mcml(seqs, clusters, type = "raw") print(cs) #> MCML Network #> ============ #> Type: raw  | Method: sum  #> Nodes: 3  | Clusters: 2  #> Transitions: 9  #>   Macro: 6  | Per-cluster: 3  #>  #> Clusters: #>   Alpha (2): A, C #>   Beta (1): B #>  #> Macro (cluster-level) weights: #>       Alpha Beta #> Alpha     3    3 #> Beta      3    0 # }

code: net <- build_network(data.frame(V1 = c("A","B","C"), V2 = c("B","C","A")),   method = "relative") boot <- bootstrap_network(net, iter = 10) print(boot) #> Bootstrap Network  [Transition Network (relative) | directed] #>   Iterations : 10  |  Nodes : 3 #>   Edges      : 0 significant / 3 total #>   CI         : 95%  |  Inference: stability  |  CR [0.75, 1.25] # \donttest{ set.seed(1) seqs <- data.frame(   V1 = c("A","B","A","C","B"), V2 = c("B","C","B","A","C"),   V3 = c("C","A","C","B","A") ) net  <- build_network(seqs, method = "relative") boot <- bootstrap_network(net, iter = 20) print(boot) #>   Edge                   Mean     95% CI          p #>   ----------------------------------------------- #>   B → C                1.000  [1.000, 1.000]  *   #>   C → A                1.000  [1.000, 1.000]  *   #>  #> Bootstrap Network  [Transition Network (relative) | directed] #>   Iterations : 20  |  Nodes : 3 #>   Edges      : 2 significant / 3 total #>   CI         : 95%  |  Inference: stability  |  CR [0.75, 1.25] # }

code: # \donttest{ # First-order Markov data: test should select order 1 set.seed(1) states <- letters[1:4] tm <- matrix(runif(16), 4, 4, dimnames = list(states, states)) tm <- tm / rowSums(tm) seqs <- lapply(1:30, function(.) {   s <- character(50); s[1] <- sample(states, 1)   for (i in 2:50) s[i] <- sample(states, 1, prob = tm[s[i - 1], ])   s }) res <- markov_order_test(seqs, max_order = 3, n_perm = 300, seed = 1) res$optimal_order #> [1] 1 summary(res) #>   order    loglik      AIC      BIC  df        g2 p_permutation p_asymptotic #> 1     0 -2011.025 4028.050 4043.990  NA        NA            NA           NA #> 2     1 -1805.112 3640.224 3719.922   9 411.78019   0.003322259 4.193315e-83 #> 3     2 -1789.863 3703.726 4033.146  36  30.35171   0.803986711 7.337874e-01 #> 4     3 -1701.468 3808.936 4887.520 136 173.56575   0.099667774 1.633824e-02 #>   significant #> 1          NA #> 2        TRUE #> 3       FALSE #> 4       FALSE plot(res)  # }

code: oh <- data.frame(A = c(1,0,1,0), B = c(0,1,0,1), C = c(1,1,0,0)) mixed <- wtna(oh, method = "both") boot  <- bootstrap_network(mixed, iter = 10) print(boot) #> Mixed Window TNA Bootstrap #> -- Transition -- #> Bootstrap Network  [Window TNA | directed] #>   Iterations : 10  |  Nodes : 3 #>   Edges      : 0 significant / 3 total #>   CI         : 95%  |  Inference: stability  |  CR [0.75, 1.25] #> -- Co-occurrence -- #> Bootstrap Network  [Network (wtna_cooccurrence) | undirected] #>   Iterations : 10  |  Nodes : 3 #>   Edges      : 0 significant / 6 total #>   CI         : 95%  |  Inference: stability  |  CR [0.75, 1.25] # \donttest{ set.seed(1) oh <- data.frame(   A = c(1,0,1,0,1,0,1,0),   B = c(0,1,0,1,0,1,0,1),   C = c(1,1,0,0,1,1,0,0) ) mixed <- wtna(oh, method = "both") boot  <- bootstrap_network(mixed, iter = 20) print(boot) #> Mixed Window TNA Bootstrap #> -- Transition -- #> Bootstrap Network  [Window TNA | directed] #>   Iterations : 20  |  Nodes : 3 #>   Edges      : 0 significant / 9 total #>   CI         : 95%  |  Inference: stability  |  CR [0.75, 1.25] #> -- Co-occurrence -- #> Bootstrap Network  [Network (wtna_cooccurrence) | undirected] #>   Iterations : 20  |  Nodes : 3 #>   Edges      : 0 significant / 6 total #>   CI         : 95%  |  Inference: stability  |  CR [0.75, 1.25] # }
